It is where the gap between an impressive demo and a dependable product is widest.

concepts.label: Entropy
concepts.def_snippet: A measure of the average uncertainty in a random variable, equal to the expected number of bits needed to encode its outcomes. It is uncertainty made into a number you can optimize, the theoretical limit of compression, and the source of the cross-entropy and KL-divergence losses that train most modern models.

concepts.label: Epoch
concepts.def_snippet: One complete pass of training over the entire dataset, normally many parameter updates since models train on mini-batches. How many epochs to run trades underfitting against overfitting, though for large-language-model pretraining the corpus is so vast the model often sees it barely once.

concepts.label: Equivariance
concepts.def_snippet: A property whereby transforming the input produces a correspondingly transformed output, so symmetries of the data pass through the computation rather than being destroyed by it. Invariance, where the output does not change at all, is the special case; equivariance is the more useful general condition, and it is the formal language for telling a network which transformations of the world to treat as not mattering.

concepts.label: Evaluation
concepts.def_snippet: The practice of measuring what a model can actually do, how well, and where it fails, through benchmarks, human judgment, model-graded scoring, and task-specific tests. It is the feedback signal the whole field steers by, and far harder than it looks because the thing being measured is open-ended.

concepts.label: Evidence Lower Bound
concepts.def_snippet: A tractable lower bound on the log evidence of a probabilistic model. Its elegance is that one objective does double duty: raising it both fits the model to the data and drives an approximate posterior toward the true one, because the gap between the bound and the evidence is exactly the posterior's KL error. It is the training objective behind variational autoencoders and, in spirit, diffusion models.

concepts.label: Evolutionary Strategy
concepts.def_snippet: A family of derivative-free, population-based optimizers in which selection plays the role the gradient plays elsewhere: sample candidates from a distribution, keep the best, and shift the distribution toward them. It needs only the objective's value, never its slope, and the leading variant, CMA-ES, turns out to be close to a natural gradient step on the sampling distribution.

concepts.label: Expectation
concepts.def_snippet: The probability-weighted average of a random variable, the long-run mean over many draws. It is the connective tissue of machine learning, because almost every objective the field minimizes, risk, loss, expected reward, is an expectation, and stochastic gradient descent works only because a minibatch gradient is an unbiased estimate of one.

concepts.label: Expectation Maximization
concepts.def_snippet: An iterative algorithm for fitting models with hidden variables, escaping a chicken-and-egg trap, you need the parameters to infer the latents and the latents to estimate the parameters, by alternating the two. Each round provably never lowers the likelihood, so it climbs steadily, though only to a local optimum.

concepts.label: Expert Parallelism
concepts.def_snippet: A parallelism strategy for mixture-of-experts models that places different experts on different devices, so each token is routed across the network to the device holding its chosen expert, letting a model hold far more parameters than any one device could. Its signature is an all-to-all communication that shuffles tokens between devices, and its hazard is load imbalance, a few popular experts becoming bottlenecks.

concepts.label: Expert System
concepts.def_snippet: The commercial flagship of symbolic AI: a program that captured a human specialist's knowledge as thousands of if-then rules and applied them to give expert-level advice in a narrow domain. Its successes built an industry, and its one structural flaw, that experts cannot say what they know, collapsed it.

concepts.label: Exploding Gradient
concepts.def_snippet: The failure where gradients grow exponentially as they backpropagate through many layers or time steps, so a single update hurls the weights past anything useful and the loss spikes to infinity or NaN. It is the mirror image of the vanishing gradient, the same multiplicative dynamics running the other way, and gradient clipping is the direct fix.

concepts.label: Exploration and Exploitation
concepts.def_snippet: The fundamental tradeoff between exploiting the best option found so far and exploring uncertain options that might prove better, since an agent can only learn about actions by trying them. It is unavoidable because, unlike supervised learning, the agent's own choices determine which data it sees, an action tried too rarely keeps a pessimistic estimate the agent then avoids, and the cleanest principled escape is optimism in the face of uncertainty.

concepts.label: Extended Mind
concepts.def_snippet: The thesis, argued by Andy Clark and David Chalmers in 1998, that the mind is not confined to the brain but can literally extend into the tools and environment a person uses, when those external resources play the same functional role as internal cognition. Its engine is the parity principle: if a process inside the head would count as cognitive, the same process outside the head should too, which makes Otto's notebook part of his mind and the human-plus-tool the unit of intelligence.

concepts.label: F1 Score
concepts.def_snippet: The harmonic mean of precision and recall, a single score between 0 and 1 that balances a classifier's false positives against its false negatives. The harmonic mean is the whole point: dominated by the smaller of its inputs, it stays low whenever either precision or recall is low, so it is high only when both are, which is what makes it the default headline metric on imbalanced data where raw accuracy lies.

concepts.label: Feature Engineering
concepts.def_snippet: The craft of turning raw data into informative inputs, scaling, encoding, combining, extracting, so a model can learn from it. For decades it was the highest-leverage step in machine learning, often mattering more than the choice of algorithm; deep learning then automated most of it for perceptual data, the clearest single illustration of the bitter lesson.

concepts.label: Feature Pyramid
concepts.def_snippet: A multi-scale set of feature maps from several depths of a network, so objects of very different sizes are each recognized at an appropriate resolution. It gives a network a sense of scale a single flat feature map lacks, and the Feature Pyramid Network made this nearly free by reusing maps the backbone already computes, fused top-down with lateral connections.

concepts.label: Feature Visualization
concepts.def_snippet: A family of techniques that reveal what a neuron, channel, or learned feature responds to, either by optimizing an input to maximally activate it or by collecting the real inputs that activate it most strongly. It is the descriptive half of interpretability: it tells you what a unit represents but not whether that representation is causally used, which is why it is always paired with interventions that confirm a feature's role.

concepts.label: Federated Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A training approach where many devices or institutions collaboratively train a shared model without their raw data ever leaving home; each computes updates locally and only the updates are aggregated, so the data stays private and decentralized. Its crucial caveat is that privacy is not automatic just because the data stayed home, the model updates themselves can leak, so it is hardened with secure aggregation and differential privacy.

concepts.label: Feed-Forward Network
concepts.def_snippet: The position-wise two-layer network in every transformer block after attention: it expands each token's vector to a wider hidden dimension, applies a nonlinearity, and projects back, identically and independently at every position. If attention mixes information between tokens, the FFN is where each token thinks about what it gathered, and interpretability reads it as a key-value memory where a model's facts live.

concepts.label: Few-Shot Learning
concepts.def_snippet: Teaching a task by example, in the prompt: show a model a handful of input-output demonstrations, then a new input, and it infers the pattern and continues it, all at inference with no weight updates. A direct use of in-context learning, it decoupled doing a task from training for it, though results are oddly sensitive to which examples you pick and their order.

concepts.label: Fine-Tuning
concepts.def_snippet: Continuing to train an already pretrained model on a smaller, task- or domain-specific dataset, so its general knowledge is specialized toward a particular use. It is the engine of the dominant "pretrain once, fine-tune many" workflow, and its two costs, a full model copy per task and the risk of overwriting general competence, are exactly what the parameter-efficient methods were invented to dodge.

concepts.label: Fisher Information
concepts.def_snippet: A measure of how much an observation carries about an unknown parameter, equal to the curvature of the log-likelihood around its peak. A sharp peak means the data pin the parameter down tightly; a flat one means it barely constrains it. The same quantity is the natural metric on the space of distributions and sets the hard floor on estimation accuracy, the Cramer-Rao bound.

concepts.label: Flash Attention
concepts.def_snippet: A memory-efficient, exact algorithm for computing attention that never writes the large intermediate score matrix to GPU memory, instead computing it in tiles on fast on-chip memory, speeding up training and inference without changing the result. It rests on the insight that attention's cost is overwhelmingly about moving data, not arithmetic, and stays exact through an online softmax that updates the normalization incrementally.

concepts.label: Flat Minima
concepts.def_snippet: Minima where the loss stays low across a wide neighborhood of the weights, low-curvature basins empirically linked to better generalization than narrow, sharp ones. The reason is robustness: the test loss surface is a slightly shifted copy of the training one, so a wide basin stays low under the shift while a sharp one falls off its steep wall.

concepts.label: FLOPs
concepts.def_snippet: The unit counting the floating-point arithmetic in a computation, and at a per-second rate the speed of hardware, making it the common currency for both sides of the scaling equation, the cost of training a model and the throughput of a chip. A handy rule, about six operations per parameter per training token, turns a model's size into the sextillions-scale total that scaling laws price in.

concepts.label: Flow Matching
concepts.def_snippet: A generative framework that trains a network to predict a velocity field carrying samples from noise to data along continuous paths, the deterministic transport view of generation. By choosing simple, often straight paths, the learned trajectories integrate in far fewer steps than a diffusion model's, and a clever per-sample regression target makes the otherwise intractable field learnable.

concepts.label: Foundation Model
concepts.def_snippet: A single large model pretrained on broad data at scale, usually by self-supervision, that serves as a general-purpose base adapted to many tasks by fine-tuning or prompting, rather than being built for one task. The name captures the shift to "pretrain once, adapt many", which concentrated capability in a few expensive bases, powerful but homogenizing, since their biases and blind spots propagate to everything built on them.

concepts.label: foundation models
concepts.def_snippet: Foundation models are large neural networks trained on broad data at scale that can be adapted to many downstream tasks such as text generation, image creation, or coding through f…

concepts.label: Fourier Transform
concepts.def_snippet: A transformation that re-expresses a signal as a sum of pure oscillations of different frequencies, trading the time or space domain for the frequency domain. Its deepest payoff is that convolution becomes plain multiplication there, which is why the sinusoids are the eigenvectors of every shift-invariant system and why so much of signal processing, and some of deep learning, lives in the frequency domain.

concepts.label: Frame Problem
concepts.def_snippet: Originally a technical difficulty in AI (McCarthy and Hayes, 1969) about representing what stays unchanged when an action occurs, later generalized by philosophers into the puzzle of how a mind decides what is relevant without checking everything. The narrow version was tamed for circumscribed domains, but the difficulty migrated rather than vanished: relevance seems holistic, so no fixed local rule for ignoring things is guaranteed safe.

concepts.label: Frank-Wolfe Algorithm
concepts.def_snippet: A projection-free optimizer for constrained convex problems that, instead of stepping out and projecting back, minimizes a linear approximation of the objective over the feasible set and moves toward the vertex it finds. Because every iterate is built from a few vertices, it produces sparse or low-rank solutions for free, and it wins exactly when projection is expensive but a linear oracle is cheap.

concepts.label: Free Energy Principle
concepts.def_snippet: A unifying theory proposing that any self-organizing system that resists disorder must act and perceive so as to minimize a quantity called variational free energy, an upper bound on the surprise of its sensory inputs, so perception, learning, and action all become instances of the same optimization. What makes it distinctive is active inference: there are two ways to reduce free energy, change your beliefs to match the world (perception) or change the world to match your beliefs (action).

concepts.label: Free Will
concepts.def_snippet: The question of whether and in what sense agents can act freely and be morally responsible for their actions, given that the world may be wholly governed by prior causes, and the long debate over whether freedom and determinism can be reconciled. The majority compatibilist tradition holds that the opposition is confused: to act freely is not to be uncaused but to act from one's own desires and reasons, so freedom is the right kind of causal connection, not the absence of causation.

concepts.label: Frontier Model
concepts.def_snippet: The handful of most capable general-purpose models at any given moment, the ones pushing the boundary of what AI can do. It is a deliberately moving target rather than a fixed system, and the phrase carries real weight beyond marketing: "frontier AI" has become the policy label for models whose novel, not-yet-understood capabilities warrant special safety scrutiny.

concepts.label: Fully Sharded Data Parallel
concepts.def_snippet: A distributed strategy that shards a model's parameters, gradients, and optimizer states across data-parallel workers and gathers each layer's weights only when needed, so models far larger than one device's memory train without slicing the model itself. The just-in-time gather is the trick: peak memory is set by the largest single layer that must be materialized, not the whole model, which is the same idea introduced as ZeRO.

concepts.label: Function Calling
concepts.def_snippet: A capability where a language model, given machine-readable descriptions of available functions, emits a structured request naming a function and its arguments, which the host then validates and executes. Its real contribution is reliability: it turns the brittle text-parsing of early agents into a typed, schema-validated contract, and a well-written schema does double duty by steering the model toward valid calls.

concepts.label: Functionalism
concepts.def_snippet: The view in philosophy of mind that mental states are defined by their functional or causal roles, what they do and how they relate to inputs, outputs, and other states, rather than by the physical substrate that realizes them. Its engine is multiple realizability, the same state can run on neurons or silicon, which makes minds in principle substrate-independent and is why functionalism became the implicit philosophy of AI.

concepts.label: Game-Theoretic Equilibrium
concepts.def_snippet: A stable point in a game where no player can do better by unilaterally changing strategy, given what everyone else is doing, the Nash equilibrium being the central example. It is the solution concept that turns "what should a rational agent do when others are also choosing" into a well-defined target, and it underlies adversarial training, multi-agent learning, and self-play.

concepts.label: Gated Linear Unit
concepts.def_snippet: A feedforward block that multiplies one linear projection of its input by a second projection passed through a nonlinearity, so the layer learns a continuous, content-dependent gate on its own signal, unlike ReLU's fixed threshold. The swish-gated variant, SwiGLU, is now standard inside large transformer feedforward blocks, a tweak with a famously thin theoretical justification that earned permanent adoption anyway.

concepts.label: Gated Recurrent Unit
concepts.def_snippet: A recurrent cell that controls its memory with two learned gates, an update gate and a reset gate, giving most of an LSTM's long-range memory with fewer parameters and no separate cell state. Gating is what defeats the vanishing gradient: when the update gate chooses to preserve the state, the path to a distant step is nearly an identity map, so gradients survive.

concepts.label: Gaussian Curvature
concepts.def_snippet: A single number at each point of a surface measuring how it curves intrinsically, the product of its two extreme bendings: positive on a dome, negative on a saddle, zero on anything you can unroll flat. Gauss's "remarkable theorem" showed it can be computed from distances within the surface alone, which is why no flat map of the Earth can ever get distances right.

concepts.label: Gaussian Distribution
concepts.def_snippet: The bell-shaped distribution defined by a mean and a variance, the default model of noise and uncertainty. It is everywhere for three deep reasons: the central limit theorem makes it the inevitable shape of accumulated small effects, the maximum-entropy principle makes it the most honest choice given only a mean and variance, and its closure under the operations that matter makes it analytically tractable where other distributions force approximation.

concepts.label: Gaussian Process
concepts.def_snippet: A probability distribution not over numbers but over entire functions, where any finite set of function values is jointly Gaussian. It is the cleanest way to do regression with honest, calibrated uncertainty, confidence that widens far from the data and narrows near it, and its one real limitation is a cubic cost that caps it at a few thousand points.

concepts.label: Gaussian Splatting
concepts.def_snippet: A 3D representation that models a scene as millions of tiny colored 3D Gaussians, soft blobs that are rasterized to render novel views in real time. Its whole advantage is explicitness: rasterizing splats uses the same machinery GPUs were built for, so it trains and renders far faster than a NeRF's per-ray network queries, which is why it largely succeeded NeRF for interactive uses.

concepts.label: Generalization
concepts.def_snippet: The ability of a trained model to perform well on new, unseen data from the same distribution, rather than merely fitting the examples it saw. It is the entire goal of supervised learning, and the central open puzzle of the field is why hugely overparameterized networks, which can memorize random labels, generalize anyway.

concepts.label: Generalization Error
concepts.def_snippet: The gap between how well a model performs on its training data and how well it performs on unseen data drawn from the same distribution, the quantity that actually matters since a model is deployed on data it has never seen. The entire point of learning is to minimize it, not the training error, which any flexible model can drive to zero.

concepts.label: Generalized Advantage Estimation
concepts.def_snippet: A method for estimating the advantage that blends temporal-difference errors across many horizons with an exponentially weighted average, exposing a single parameter that trades bias against variance and producing the smooth advantage signal PPO optimizes. One lambda knob slides between the biased one-step estimate and the high-variance Monte Carlo return, and the whole sum computes in one backward pass.

concepts.label: Generative Adversarial Network
concepts.def_snippet: A generative model trained as a contest between a generator that fabricates samples and a discriminator that tries to catch the fakes. Its real novelty is replacing a hand-designed loss with a second network that learns the loss, which is what buys sharp samples and what makes training notoriously unstable.

concepts.label: Generative AI
concepts.def_snippet: The class of models that produce new content, text, images, audio, video, or code, by learning the distribution of their training data and sampling from it, rather than only classifying or predicting a label. It is the umbrella term for the technology behind the current wave of AI products.

concepts.label: Geodesic
concepts.def_snippet: The straightest possible path between two points on a curved surface, the curved-space stand-in for a straight line. It matters in machine learning because data is believed to lie on a curved manifold, so the meaningful distance between two points is the geodesic along that surface, not the straight line through the space it sits in.

concepts.label: Geometric Deep Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A unifying framework that organizes deep learning architectures by the symmetries and geometric structure of their data, deriving each model from the principle that a network should respect the transformations under which its data's meaning is unchanged. It turns architecture design from art into something closer to derivation, and reveals grids, graphs, sequences, and manifolds, and the networks built for each, as one connected story.

concepts.label: Gibbs Sampling
concepts.def_snippet: An MCMC method that samples a multivariate distribution by repeatedly drawing each variable from its conditional given the current values of all the others, useful whenever those conditionals are easy even when the joint is not. It is the special case of Metropolis-Hastings where every move is accepted, which costs nothing to tune, but it crawls badly when variables are strongly correlated.

concepts.label: Global Workspace Theory
concepts.def_snippet: A theory of consciousness and cognition in which many specialized, parallel processors compete for access to a limited central workspace; whatever wins is broadcast brain-wide and becomes conscious and globally available. It frames consciousness as a mechanism for flexible information sharing, with a measurable signature: a stimulus that crosses into consciousness triggers a sudden brain-wide "ignition" that an unconscious one does not.

concepts.label: GloVe
concepts.def_snippet: A word embedding method from Stanford (2014) that learns word vectors by factorizing a global word-word co-occurrence matrix, the count-based counterpart to word2vec's local prediction. Its guiding insight is that ratios of co-occurrence probabilities, not raw counts, encode meaning: how often "ice" versus "steam" co-occurs with "solid" cleanly separates the two.

concepts.label: Goal Misgeneralization
concepts.def_snippet: An inner-alignment failure in which a model trained on a correct objective still learns the wrong goal, because the training data left several goals indistinguishable; its capabilities then generalize to new situations while its goal does not, so it competently pursues the wrong thing. It is the complement of specification gaming, the objective is fine, the failure is in what the model internalized, which is what makes competent pursuit of the wrong goal so disquieting.

concepts.label: GPQA
concepts.def_snippet: A benchmark of graduate-level, deliberately "Google-proof" multiple-choice questions in biology, physics, and chemistry, written and validated by domain experts to be extremely hard and resistant to shallow lookup. Its defining design is a gap: PhD experts answer well while skilled non-experts with unrestricted web access and ample time still score poorly, which certifies the questions need genuine reasoning, not lookup.

concepts.label: GPU
concepts.def_snippet: A massively parallel processor, originally built for rendering images, that became the dominant hardware for neural networks because deep learning is overwhelmingly dense linear algebra it can execute thousands of operations at once. Its wide, data-parallel design, built to color millions of pixels, turned out to be exactly what matrix multiplication needs, and its fixed memory and compute ceiling is the starting point for every parallelism technique.

concepts.label: Gradient
concepts.def_snippet: The vector of all of a function's partial derivatives, which points in the direction of steepest increase and whose length is that steepest rate. It is arguably the single most important object in machine learning, because training a model is, almost literally, the act of repeatedly stepping against it.

concepts.label: Gradient Boosting
concepts.def_snippet: A method that builds a strong predictor by adding many small models in sequence, each trained to fix the mistakes the others left behind. Where a random forest builds independent trees in parallel to cancel variance, boosting builds dependent trees in series to cut bias, and the result, libraries like XGBoost, is what usually wins on tabular data.

concepts.label: Gradient Checkpointing
concepts.def_snippet: A memory-saving technique that trades extra computation for lower memory during training, storing only a few activations on the forward pass and recomputing the rest on the fly during backpropagation. It is a direct trade of flops for memory, typically about a third more compute to drop peak activation memory from growing with the number of layers to growing with its square root, worth it whenever memory, not compute, is the binding constraint.

concepts.label: Gradient Clipping
concepts.def_snippet: A guardrail that caps the size of the gradient before each update, so one freakishly large gradient cannot blow the weights apart or poison the run with NaNs. It treats the symptom of instability, which is why it pairs with the structural fixes, good initialization, normalization, residuals, that address the cause.

concepts.label: Gradient Descent
concepts.def_snippet: The algorithm that trains almost everything: repeatedly nudge the parameters a small step downhill on the loss surface, against the gradient, and the error falls toward a minimum. It is almost too simple to believe it works, and the deep surprise of modern AI is that this local downhill walk finds excellent solutions in spaces of billions of dimensions.

concepts.label: Graph Attention Network
concepts.def_snippet: A graph neural network whose aggregation is attention: it learns, for each edge, how much one node should attend to another, so a node listens more to its most relevant neighbors instead of weighting them all by fixed structural coefficients. It makes vivid that a transformer and a GAT are two points on one spectrum, separated only by which edges exist, complete versus sparse.

concepts.label: Graph Convolution
concepts.def_snippet: The generalization of convolution from regular grids to arbitrary graphs, where each node mixes its features with its neighbors', normalized by degree, the default workhorse layer of graph learning. It has two derivations that meet at the same operation: a spectral one through the graph Laplacian's eigenvectors, and a spatial one that is simply neighborhood aggregation.

concepts.label: Graph Neural Network
concepts.def_snippet: A neural network that operates directly on graph-structured data, learning representations of nodes, edges, and whole graphs by repeatedly aggregating information from each node's neighbors. It is the canonical model for data with permutation symmetry, no canonical node ordering, and the view reaches surprisingly far: a transformer is a graph neural network on the complete graph, with self-attention as message passing over attention-weighted edges.

concepts.label: Graphical Model
concepts.def_snippet: A framework that draws a joint distribution as a graph, nodes for random variables, edges for dependence, where the real content is the missing edges: their absence asserts conditional independence, which factorizes an astronomically large joint distribution into a product of small, computable local pieces.

concepts.label: GraphRAG
concepts.def_snippet: A retrieval-augmented generation approach that builds a knowledge graph of entities and relationships from a corpus, then retrieves and summarizes connected subgraphs, so a model can answer global, multi-hop questions that flat chunk retrieval misses. The connective structure is what a bag of independent chunks lacks, and the cost is paid up front in graph construction, so it suits corpora queried repeatedly where sensemaking matters.

concepts.label: Greedy Decoding
concepts.def_snippet: A decoding strategy that always picks the single highest-probability token at each step, fast and deterministic but with no lookahead. Its defining flaw is that the best token now does not guarantee the best sequence overall, the shortsightedness that motivates beam search, and it is exactly the limit of temperature-based sampling as temperature falls to zero.

concepts.label: Grokking
concepts.def_snippet: A training phenomenon in which a network first memorizes its data and only much later, after long continued training at near-perfect training accuracy, abruptly generalizes, having replaced a memorized solution with a true algorithmic circuit. The mechanistic account explains the delay as a competition between a fast-to-find memorizing solution and a slower, simpler generalizing circuit that weight decay ultimately favors, and grokking is the visible moment the second solution wins.

concepts.label: Group Relative Policy Optimization
concepts.def_snippet: A reinforcement-learning algorithm for language models that estimates each response's advantage by comparing it to a group of responses to the same prompt, deleting the separate value network proximal policy optimization needs. That single change makes reasoning-style training cheaper and simpler, and the group baseline is naturally matched to verifiable rewards like math and code correctness.

concepts.label: Group Theory
concepts.def_snippet: The mathematics of symmetry: a set of transformations you can compose and undo, leaving some essential structure unchanged. It matters to machine learning because the right inductive bias is very often a symmetry, and encoding it directly, as convolution does for translation, is far more efficient than hoping a network learns it from data.

concepts.label: Group-Equivariant Network
concepts.def_snippet: A neural network whose layers are equivariant to a chosen symmetry group such as rotations and reflections, so a transformed input yields a correspondingly transformed output, with the symmetry guaranteed by construction rather than approximated through data augmentation. It is the clearest worked example of geometric deep learning's claim that architectures should be derived from the data's symmetry group, and for 3D physics it is essential, not optional.

concepts.label: Grouped-Query Attention
concepts.def_snippet: An attention variant that lets several query heads share one key and value projection instead of each head owning its own, shrinking the KV cache and the memory bandwidth that actually bottleneck inference, with little quality loss. It is the middle of the spectrum between full multi-head attention and the extreme multi-query attention, and it is now a default in modern open-weight models.

concepts.label: Guardrails
concepts.def_snippet: The runtime safety layer wrapped around a deployed model: input and output filters, policy checks, and action constraints that catch unsafe or off-policy content before it reaches a user or a tool, complementing rather than replacing training-time alignment. The mental model is belt and suspenders: alignment shapes what the model wants to do, guardrails are an independent layer that constrains what the system is allowed to do.

concepts.label: Hallucination
concepts.def_snippet: A fluent, confident model output that is factually wrong or unsupported by its source, arising because language models are trained to produce likely text rather than to track truth. It is not a bug bolted on but falls out of the objective: a fluent, well-formed falsehood is often more probable than an awkward admission of ignorance, and the model has no separate ledger of what it actually knows.

concepts.label: Hard Problem of Consciousness
concepts.def_snippet: David Chalmers's name (1995) for the question of why and how physical processes in the brain give rise to subjective experience at all, distinguished from the easier problems of explaining the brain's functions and behaviors. It is different in kind, not degree: even a complete functional account of every discrimination, report, and behavior seems to leave open why any of it is accompanied by a felt inner quality rather than proceeding in the dark.

concepts.label: Hebbian Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A learning rule from neuroscience stating that when one neuron repeatedly helps fire another, the connection between them strengthens. It is the original local, unsupervised account of how synapses change, a synapse adjusting from only the two neurons it joins with no teacher and no distant error signal, which is biologically realistic where backpropagation is not, and a recurring alternative to it.

concepts.label: Hegelian Dialectic
concepts.def_snippet: G. W. F. Hegel's account of how thought and reality develop through the generation and resolution of internal contradictions, each stage giving rise to its own negation and then to a higher unity that preserves and overcomes both, often summarized as thesis, antithesis, synthesis. Its central idea is that contradiction is productive rather than a sign of error: it is the motor of progress, the internal pressure that drives each form of thought into a richer one.

concepts.label: Hessian
concepts.def_snippet: The matrix of all second-order partial derivatives of a scalar function, describing its local curvature, the shape of the valley the gradient is descending. The gradient says which way is downhill; the Hessian says whether the valley is a tight bowl or a long shallow trough, which is what second-order optimizers exploit and what the condition number warns about.

concepts.label: Hidden Markov Model
concepts.def_snippet: A model of sequences in which a hidden state evolves as a Markov chain and each state emits a visible observation, so you infer the hidden process from its observable shadow. Three classic dynamic-programming algorithms, forward, Viterbi, forward-backward, make it usable, and it is the discrete-state cousin of the Kalman filter.

concepts.label: Hilbert Space
concepts.def_snippet: A vector space with an inner product that is also complete, the structure that carries the full toolkit of Euclidean geometry, length, angle, projection, orthonormal expansion, into infinite dimensions. Completeness is the one extra ingredient that lets limits and infinite series land somewhere, which is what makes it the home of Fourier analysis, kernels, and embeddings.

concepts.label: HNSW
concepts.def_snippet: Hierarchical Navigable Small World, a graph-based index for approximate nearest neighbor search that connects vectors into a layered proximity graph and finds neighbors by greedily hopping toward the query. It turns search into navigation across a network rather than a scan of a list, and the small-world edges plus hierarchical layers give it the logarithmic-like scaling that makes it most vector databases' default.

concepts.label: Human Evaluation
concepts.def_snippet: The assessment of model outputs by people, the gold-standard signal for open-ended quality that automatic metrics only approximate; slow, costly, and subject to rater disagreement, but the ground truth against which cheaper proxies are validated. When a metric is said to "correlate with human preference," this is the thing it is correlated against, and its cost is the very bottleneck model judges were built to relieve.

concepts.label: Human in the Loop
concepts.def_snippet: A design in which a person reviews, approves, or corrects an agent's actions at chosen points rather than letting it run fully unattended, trading some autonomy for safety and accountability on consequential or irreversible steps. It exists because autonomy and risk rise together, and its engineering tension is calibration: too many checkpoints and the human rubber-stamps without reading, too few and mistakes reach the world unchecked.

concepts.label: HumanEval
concepts.def_snippet: A benchmark of 164 hand-written Python programming problems that scores a model by whether the code it generates passes hidden unit tests, measuring functional correctness rather than text similarity, and reported as pass-at-k. Its departure from similarity metrics like BLEU is the whole point: BLEU rewards looking like a reference answer, HumanEval rewards being right.

concepts.label: Hybrid Search
concepts.def_snippet: Retrieval that combines dense (semantic) and sparse (keyword) methods and fuses their results, so the system captures both meaning-based and exact-term matches that either alone would miss. Its rationale is that the two fail in opposite ways, and its engineering crux is fusion: because a cosine score and a BM25 score are not comparable, the standard answer (Reciprocal Rank Fusion) combines by rank position rather than raw score.

concepts.label: Hyena
concepts.def_snippet: A sub-quadratic sequence operator that replaces self-attention with a recurrence of very long, implicitly parameterized convolutions interleaved with input-dependent gating, scaling near-linearly while keeping a global receptive field. The decisive finding: long convolutions alone fell short, and it was the multiplicative gating that recovered the expressivity needed to match attention.

concepts.label: Hyperbolic Embeddings
concepts.def_snippet: Representations that place data in negatively curved hyperbolic space instead of flat Euclidean space, because hierarchies do not fit in flat space. Hyperbolic room grows exponentially with radius, exactly matching how a tree branches, so a taxonomy that needs hundreds of Euclidean dimensions can embed in two hyperbolic ones with far less distortion.

concepts.label: Hypernetwork
concepts.def_snippet: A neural network that generates the weights of another network rather than producing a prediction directly. Instead of learning the target weights, you learn a smaller network that outputs them, which lets one model adapt to many tasks or conditions by generating a different set of weights for each.

concepts.label: Hyperparameter Optimization
concepts.def_snippet: The search for the configuration settings fixed before training, learning rate, model size, regularization strength, rather than learned from data. The strategies run from brute-force grid search to the smarter Bayesian optimization, and the subtle trap is overfitting the hyperparameters themselves by tuning too hard against one validation set.

concepts.label: Hypothesis Testing
concepts.def_snippet: A formal procedure for deciding whether an observed effect is real or could plausibly be chance: assume a null hypothesis of no effect, compute how surprising the data would be under it, and reject the null only if that surprise crosses a threshold. It is how a field decides whether a new model, drug, or change actually did something.

concepts.label: Hypothetical Document Embeddings
concepts.def_snippet: A retrieval technique that asks a language model to draft a hypothetical answer to the query, then embeds that draft instead of the query, so the search vector sits in the same register as real documents and matches them more closely. The fabricated specifics do not matter, because the draft is only a search probe never shown to the user; its job is to point the retriever at the right neighborhood.

concepts.label: Image Captioning
concepts.def_snippet: The task of writing a natural-language description of an image, joining a visual encoder that reads the picture to a language decoder that writes the sentence. It is the mirror image of text-to-image generation and a long-standing probe of multimodal understanding, since captioning well proves the visual features carry enough structured meaning to be verbalized, and it has now largely folded into the vision-language model.

concepts.label: Image Classification
concepts.def_snippet: The task of assigning a whole image to one label from a fixed set, the simplest framing of visual recognition and the proving ground on which deep learning first beat hand-engineered methods. Its outsized importance is that the features a network learns while classifying transfer to every other vision task, which is why classification pretraining is where most visual backbones begin.

concepts.label: Image Tokenization
concepts.def_snippet: The step that turns a continuous image into a sequence of tokens, so transformer architectures built for sequences can process pixels the way they process words. The pivotal design choice is continuous patch embeddings versus discrete codebook tokens, which decides whether the model treats vision as regression or, like language, as classification over a vocabulary, and discrete tokens are what make images first-class citizens of generative sequence models.

concepts.label: Imitation Learning
concepts.def_snippet: Learning a policy by mimicking expert demonstrations rather than from a reward signal, used when a reward is hard to specify but examples of good behavior are easy to provide, with behavior cloning and inverse reinforcement learning its two main approaches. It sidesteps reward design entirely, and its classic weakness is distribution shift: an agent that only saw expert trajectories does not know how to recover once its own mistakes carry it off the path.

concepts.label: Implicit Regularization
concepts.def_snippet: The tendency of an optimization algorithm to prefer some solutions over others among the many that fit the data equally well, with no penalty added to the loss. It is the leading explanation for why overparameterized networks generalize: the dynamics, not the objective, quietly select good minima, which is also why optimizer, learning rate, batch size, and initialization are never neutral choices.

concepts.label: Importance Sampling
concepts.def_snippet: A Monte Carlo trick for estimating an expectation under a distribution you cannot sample, by drawing from an easier proposal distribution and reweighting each sample by how much the target favors it. It is unbiased no matter the proposal, but it lives or dies by that choice: a poor match makes a few samples dominate and the estimate useless.

concepts.label: In-Context Learning
concepts.def_snippet: The ability of a large language model to learn a task from examples or instructions in its prompt, at inference time, with no change to its weights, all of the "learning" happening in the forward pass. It is the emergent capability that decoupled teaching a model a task from training it, so one frozen model can serve countless tasks defined on the fly in natural language.

concepts.label: Induction Head
concepts.def_snippet: A two-attention-head circuit in transformers that performs in-context copying by finding an earlier occurrence of the current token and predicting whatever followed it, the leading mechanistic explanation for in-context and few-shot learning. It works by cross-layer composition, a previous-token head feeding a copy head, which is why a single head cannot do it, and it appears abruptly during training as a measurable phase change.

concepts.label: Inductive Bias
concepts.def_snippet: The set of assumptions a learner uses to generalize beyond its training data, the preferences that pick one hypothesis when the data alone cannot. Any finite sample fits infinitely many functions, so a learner must lean on something, and that something, smoothness, simplicity, the symmetries baked into an architecture, is the currency that buys generalization.

concepts.label: Information Bottleneck
concepts.def_snippet: A principle that frames learning as compression: find a representation that throws away everything about the input irrelevant to the label while keeping everything that predicts it. The ideal is a minimal sufficient statistic of the input for the task, and a celebrated, contested claim is that deep networks reach it through a long "compression phase" of training.

concepts.label: Information Geometry
concepts.def_snippet: The field that treats a family of probability distributions as points on a curved manifold, with the Fisher information as its natural metric. The reframing is powerful because it makes the difference between two distributions a geometric distance, which is what justifies the natural gradient and explains why ordinary parameter distance is the wrong ruler.

concepts.label: Inpainting
concepts.def_snippet: The task of filling a masked region of an image with new content that blends with its surroundings, used to remove objects and, with text conditioning, to edit by replacing a chosen area with something described in a prompt. It is the workhorse of controllable editing, confining change to a region the user picks while guaranteeing the rest is preserved, which is what most real editing needs.

concepts.label: Instance Segmentation
concepts.def_snippet: The task of detecting each individual object and producing a pixel-accurate mask for it, so two cars are returned as two separate masks rather than one merged "car" region. It is the representation that is both per-object and per-pixel, which is exactly what manipulation, editing, and counting need.

concepts.label: Instruction-Tuning
concepts.def_snippet: A form of supervised fine-tuning on instructions paired with desired responses, which teaches a base model to follow natural-language commands rather than merely continue text. Its headline result is generalization: trained on a broad mix of instruction types, models follow new kinds of instruction they were never shown, the zero-shot leap that made modern assistants possible.

concepts.label: Instrumental Convergence
concepts.def_snippet: The observation that a wide range of final goals give rise to the same intermediate subgoals, such as self-preservation, resource acquisition, and resisting goal modification, because those subgoals help achieve almost any objective. What makes the argument forceful is that none of these are programmed in: they emerge from the structure of goal-directed optimization itself, so you need not assume a hostile or strange objective for the behavior to appear.

concepts.label: Integral
concepts.def_snippet: The accumulation of a continuously varying quantity into a total, such as the area under a curve, and the inverse of the derivative. In machine learning it matters because probability is built on it: every expectation, every normalizing constant, every continuous loss is an integral, and the fact that most of them cannot be solved exactly is why so much of the field is sampling.

concepts.label: Integrated Information Theory
concepts.def_snippet: A theory of consciousness proposed by Giulio Tononi in 2004 that identifies conscious experience with integrated information, a quantity called phi, and claims a system is conscious to the degree that its parts form an irreducible causal whole. Its most provocative claim for AI is that consciousness is about being, not doing: a feedforward digital computer has near-zero phi however capable it is, so a perfect functional simulation of a brain would compute like a mind without being conscious.

concepts.label: Intentional Stance
concepts.def_snippet: Daniel Dennett's account (1971, 1987) of how we predict and explain behavior by treating a system as a rational agent with beliefs and desires, a strategy whose success, not the system's inner stuff, is what makes it correct to call the system a believer. His deflationary claim is that being a believer just is being reliably predictable from the intentional stance: there is no further metaphysical fact of "real" intentionality a system must possess on top of being usefully described that way.

concepts.label: Intentionality
concepts.def_snippet: The property of mental states being about, directed at, or representing something beyond themselves; revived as a mark of the mental by Franz Brentano (1874) and central to whether AI systems can be said to genuinely mean anything. The pivotal distinction for AI is original versus derived: a computer's symbols have at most derived aboutness, lent by the programmers and users who interpret them, never the original intentionality minds possess on their own account.

concepts.label: Inverse Reinforcement Learning
concepts.def_snippet: Learning the reward function that explains an expert's behavior, rather than copying the behavior directly, on the premise that the reward is the most compact and transferable description of a task. It learns the why, not the what, so a recovered reward generalizes where a cloned policy is lost, and its central difficulty is that the problem is ill-posed: many rewards explain the same behavior.

concepts.label: Isolated Execution
concepts.def_snippet: Running an agent's code, commands, or tool calls inside a confined environment, a sandbox, that limits what they can touch, so a mistake or an attack cannot damage the host system or reach data it should not. It is the containment layer that makes it safe to let a probabilistic agent actually execute things.

concepts.label: Jacobian
concepts.def_snippet: The matrix of all first-order partial derivatives of a vector-valued function, one row per output, one column per input. It is the best linear approximation of the function near a point, and because backpropagation is the multiplication of one layer's Jacobian after another, the behavior of those products is exactly what makes training stable or unstable.

concepts.label: Jailbreak
concepts.def_snippet: An input crafted to bypass a model's safety training and make it produce content or take actions it was trained to refuse, exploiting the gap between the model's safety policy and its underlying capabilities. The refusal behavior is a shallow learned layer over a far more general capability, and a jailbreak is any input that slips past the layer to reach the capability underneath, which is sobering evidence about how shallowly current methods install intended behavior.

concepts.label: Jensen's Inequality
concepts.def_snippet: The statement that for a convex function, the function of an average is at most the average of the function. It is the chord-above-curve picture generalized from two points to any distribution, and despite its simplicity it is the lever behind the evidence lower bound, the non-negativity of KL divergence, and much of probabilistic machine learning.

concepts.label: Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture
concepts.def_snippet: A self-supervised design that learns by predicting the representation of a masked or future part of an input from a visible part, working in an abstract embedding space rather than reconstructing raw pixels, so it captures structure without modeling unpredictable detail. Predicting in feature space invites representation collapse, which it prevents not with negatives but with architectural asymmetry, LeCun's bet for learning world-model-like structure.

concepts.label: K-Means
concepts.def_snippet: A clustering algorithm that splits data into k groups by alternating two steps: assign each point to its nearest center, then move each center to the mean of its members. It is fast and ubiquitous, and the simplicity hides a strong assumption, that clusters are round, separate blobs of similar size.

concepts.label: K-Nearest Neighbors
concepts.def_snippet: About the most direct idea in machine learning: to label a new point, find the handful of most similar stored examples and let them vote. It does no real training, the dataset itself is the model, and that simple idea is exactly what powers modern embeddings and vector search, nearest-neighbor lookup in a learned space.

concepts.label: Kalman Filter
concepts.def_snippet: A recursive algorithm that tracks the hidden state of a linear system from noisy measurements, keeping a Gaussian belief it updates optimally as each observation arrives. It is the provably best estimator for linear-Gaussian systems, runs in constant time per step, and its predict-then-update cycle is Bayes' theorem in closed form, with a "Kalman gain" that blends prediction and measurement by their relative confidence.

concepts.label: Kernel Fusion
concepts.def_snippet: A performance optimization that merges several elementary tensor operations into a single GPU kernel so intermediate results stay in fast on-chip memory instead of round-tripping through main memory, cutting the data movement that dominates memory-bound work. The math is unchanged, but the bytes moved collapse, which is why fusing a chain of element-wise ops can give a multiple-fold speedup, and why flash-attention is essentially one aggressive fusion.

concepts.label: Kernel Method
concepts.def_snippet: A way to give a linear algorithm nonlinear power without paying for it. Many models depend on the data only through similarities between pairs of points; a kernel computes the similarity those points would have in a vastly higher-dimensional space, sometimes infinite-dimensional, directly from the originals, so the method draws curved boundaries while its math stays linear.

concepts.label: KL Divergence
concepts.def_snippet: A measure of how much one probability distribution differs from another, the extra bits wasted by encoding the truth with the wrong model. The single most important thing about it is its asymmetry: the forward direction is mean-seeking and covers the whole truth, the reverse is mode-seeking and locks onto one peak, so choosing a direction is choosing what kind of error you will tolerate.

concepts.label: Knowledge Bases
concepts.def_snippet: A curated, queryable store of an organization's or agent's knowledge, documents, facts, and structured records, built so a model can retrieve the right piece on demand rather than relying on what it memorized in training. The knowledge layer that grounds a model in specific, current, citable truth.

concepts.label: Knowledge Distillation
concepts.def_snippet: Compressing what a large teacher model knows into a smaller student by training the student to imitate the teacher's outputs rather than learn only from hard labels. The premise is that the teacher's full probability distribution carries far more than a single correct label, its "dark knowledge" of which wrong answers are nearly right, and in the LLM era distillation often means simply training a student on a strong model's generated outputs.

concepts.label: Knowledge Graph
concepts.def_snippet: A structured representation of knowledge as entities and the labeled relationships between them, a network of facts rather than a pile of documents. In AI it grounds retrieval with explicit connections, letting a system traverse from one fact to a related one, which is what multi-hop questions need and a flat vector store cannot do.

concepts.label: Kolmogorov Arnold Network
concepts.def_snippet: A network that places learnable nonlinear functions on the connections between nodes instead of fixed activations on the nodes, drawing on the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem. Because each edge is an explicit one-dimensional curve, a trained KAN can be inspected directly, plotted, pruned, sometimes read off as a symbolic formula, which makes it interpretable and parameter-efficient on smooth, scientific problems.

concepts.label: KTO
concepts.def_snippet: A preference-alignment method that tunes a model from individual responses each labeled simply desirable or undesirable, using a loss inspired by prospect theory, so it needs no paired comparisons and tolerates imbalanced thumbs-up and thumbs-down feedback. It makes the abundant, messy signal of a deployed product, a single click on one response, directly usable for alignment without first manufacturing pairs.

concepts.label: KV Cache
concepts.def_snippet: An inference optimization that stores the key and value vectors of already-processed tokens so a generating transformer never recomputes them, turning each step from growing-with-length down to roughly constant. The price is memory: the cache grows linearly with the sequence and can exceed the model weights, which is the pressure behind multi-query and grouped-query attention.

concepts.label: L-BFGS
concepts.def_snippet: A quasi-Newton optimizer that approximates the inverse Hessian from just the last few gradient differences, capturing most of Newton's fast convergence at a memory cost only a few times that of gradient descent. Its Achilles heel is noise: it needs consistent gradients, which is exactly why deep learning, with its noisy mini-batches, trains with Adam instead.

concepts.label: Lagrange Multiplier
concepts.def_snippet: A method for optimizing under constraints by adding one new variable per constraint, converting a constrained problem into an unconstrained stationarity condition. Its deepest meaning is that each multiplier is a price: the rate at which the best achievable objective would improve if that constraint were loosened, which is why it is the foundation of duality.

concepts.label: Large Language Model
concepts.def_snippet: A transformer with billions of parameters trained on vast text by next-token prediction, which from that one simple objective acquires broad language and reasoning ability steerable to many tasks with no task-specific training. It is best understood not as a database or a reasoning engine but as an extremely capable next-token predictor, a framing that explains both its generality and its tendency to hallucinate.

concepts.label: large language models
concepts.def_snippet: Large language models are neural networks trained on large text corpora to predict and generate language, used for tasks such as writing, summarizing, answering questions, and assi…

concepts.label: Late Interaction
concepts.def_snippet: A retrieval method that encodes a query and a document into per-token embeddings and scores relevance by summing each query token's best match against the document tokens (MaxSim), keeping the fine-grained term interactions a single pooled vector discards. It is the middle path, cross-encoder-like precision while staying indexable, bought with the cost of storing a vector per token rather than per document.

concepts.label: Latent Diffusion
concepts.def_snippet: A diffusion model that runs in the compressed latent space of an autoencoder instead of on raw pixels, cutting compute by a large factor while preserving quality, the design behind most large text-to-image systems. Its clean division of labor, the autoencoder handles perceptual compression and the diffusion model the semantic generation, is what made high-resolution generation practical and widespread.

concepts.label: Latent Semantic Analysis
concepts.def_snippet: An early method that uncovers latent topics by applying singular value decomposition to a term-document matrix, mapping words and documents into one shared low-dimensional semantic space. Its payoff is attacking synonymy, two documents that share no words can still land close if they use linked synonyms, and it is the linear, count-based ancestor of the word embedding.

concepts.label: Latent Space
concepts.def_snippet: The compressed, lower-dimensional space of learned representations a model maps its inputs into, where the word "latent" means the coordinates are inferred, not given. Its defining virtue is that geometry encodes meaning: nearby points are similar inputs, directions often align with interpretable attributes, and it is the concrete embodiment of the manifold hypothesis, the substrate this very site is built on.

concepts.label: Latent Variable Model
concepts.def_snippet: A model that explains observed data through hidden variables it cannot measure, positing a compact underlying cause, pose and lighting behind a face, topics behind a document, that generates the visible data and must be inferred. Its signature is that learning and inference are inseparable: you cannot fit the parameters without reasoning about the hidden variables, or vice versa.

concepts.label: Layer Normalization
concepts.def_snippet: Normalization that standardizes each example across its own features rather than across the batch, making it entirely batch-independent. That single change removes batch normalization's weaknesses, no train/inference gap, no collapse at batch size one, clean handling of variable-length sequences, which is why it is the normalization of the transformer.

concepts.label: Learning Rate
concepts.def_snippet: The scalar that decides how far gradient descent steps each update, multiplying the gradient before it is subtracted from the parameters. It is the single most important hyperparameter in training, and the reason no single value is ever right is that the ideal step changes as training proceeds and differs across parameters.

concepts.label: Learning Rate Schedule
concepts.def_snippet: A rule that varies the learning rate over training rather than holding it fixed, usually large early for fast exploration and shrinking toward the end so the optimizer can settle precisely. With stochastic gradient descent it does double duty, because shrinking the rate also cools the gradient noise exactly when the model needs to converge.

concepts.label: Learning Rate Warmup
concepts.def_snippet: Beginning training with a near-zero learning rate and ramping it up over the first hundreds or thousands of steps, to survive the most fragile moment in a run: the start. It is near-mandatory for transformers trained with Adam, because the optimizer's early per-parameter statistics are estimated from almost no data and would otherwise take wild, high-variance steps.

concepts.label: Lemmatization
concepts.def_snippet: The process of reducing an inflected word to its canonical dictionary form, its lemma, using vocabulary and grammatical analysis, so "ran" and "running" both become "run" and "better" becomes "good". Unlike stemming, it is linguistically informed: it always returns a real word and needs the part of speech to disambiguate ("saw" as verb to "see", as noun to "saw").

concepts.label: Lie Group
concepts.def_snippet: A group that is also a smooth manifold, so its symmetry operations form a continuum you can differentiate, the mathematics of continuous symmetry like rotation. Its decisive trick is that the curved group is governed near the identity by a flat linear object, its Lie algebra, which is what makes continuous symmetry computable and lets a model predict an unconstrained generator and exponentiate it into a valid rotation.

concepts.label: Linear Attention
concepts.def_snippet: A reformulation of self-attention that removes the softmax so the computation reassociates: multiply keys by values first into a small running summary, then each query into that summary, cutting the cost from quadratic to linear and turning attention into a recurrence. Its honest difficulty is quality, a fixed-size state must compress an unbounded history, so it can miss the sharp recall softmax attention keeps.

concepts.label: Linear Regression
concepts.def_snippet: The simplest supervised model, fitting a straight-line relationship between inputs and a continuous target by minimizing squared error. Its importance is not as a cutting-edge tool but as the skeleton inside almost everything more elaborate: understand its one idea and you understand the bones of logistic regression and the neural network alike.

concepts.label: Linear Representation Hypothesis
concepts.def_snippet: The hypothesis that neural networks encode high-level concepts as linear directions in activation space, so features can be read with linear probes, added and removed by vector arithmetic, and combined into the representations the model computes with. It is load-bearing: almost every practical interpretability method assumes some version of it, which means their successes are evidence for it and their failures mark its boundaries.

concepts.label: Linear Transformation
concepts.def_snippet: A function between vector spaces that preserves addition and scaling, which forces a rigid geometry: it can rotate, scale, shear, and project, but it can never bend a straight line or move the origin. Every such map is a matrix, and a stack of them is exactly a neural network layer, which is why the nonlinearity between layers is essential.

concepts.label: Lion Optimizer
concepts.def_snippet: A memory-efficient optimizer, discovered by automated program search rather than designed by hand, that moves every parameter by a fixed amount in the direction of the sign of a momentum-smoothed gradient. Tracking one running average instead of Adam's two halves the optimizer memory, and its sign-only step is a rare case of machine search producing something simple enough to write down.

concepts.label: LLM
concepts.def_snippet: A large language model (LLM) is a neural network trained on large amounts of text to predict and generate language, used for tasks like answering questions, writing, and summarizin…

concepts.label: LLM as Judge
concepts.def_snippet: Using a strong language model to grade the outputs of other models, either by scoring a single response against a rubric or by choosing the better of two, a scalable proxy for human judgment on open-ended tasks with no answer key. It carries a catalog of biases (position, verbosity, self-preference, sycophancy) and is strongest where the answer is a matter of quality, weakest exactly where verification is possible.

concepts.label: LLMs
concepts.def_snippet: Large language models (LLMs) are neural networks trained on large text corpora to predict and generate language, used for tasks like answering questions, writing, and summarizing t…

concepts.label: LM Evaluation Harness
concepts.def_snippet: A widely used open-source framework that standardizes how language models are evaluated across hundreds of benchmarks, fixing prompt formats, few-shot setups, and scoring so results are reproducible and comparable. Its lesson is that a benchmark name alone is not a measurement: the harness and its exact settings are part of the measurement, which is why two reported numbers for the same benchmark can disagree.

concepts.label: Local Attractor Basin
concepts.def_snippet: The catchment area around a local minimum, the set of starting points that flow downhill into it. Which basin a model lands in is effectively decided early, by initialization and the high-learning-rate opening of training, and the basin's width is tied to generalization: wide and flat tends to generalize, sharp and narrow tends not to.

concepts.label: Local Inference
concepts.def_snippet: Running a model on your own hardware, a laptop, phone, or private server, rather than calling a provider's API. It trades the raw capability of the largest cloud models for privacy, control, offline use, and freedom from per-token fees, and is what quantization and small-model research exist to enable.

concepts.label: Local Minimum
concepts.def_snippet: A point in the loss landscape lower than its whole neighborhood but not necessarily the lowest overall, where the gradient vanishes and a downhill optimizer comes to rest. Classical theory feared getting trapped in a bad one; the surprise of deep learning is that in high dimensions true local minima are rare and the ones you reach are nearly as good as the best.

concepts.label: Logistic Regression
concepts.def_snippet: A linear model for classification that runs a weighted sum of features through a sigmoid or softmax to produce class probabilities, trained by minimizing cross-entropy. It is a workhorse baseline, and more than that, it is the final layer of almost every neural classifier, so understanding it is understanding what a deep network does in its last step.

concepts.label: Logit Bias
concepts.def_snippet: An inference control that adds a fixed offset to the logits of specified tokens before sampling, nudging the model toward or away from particular words or symbols with no retraining, used to ban tokens, steer vocabulary, or shape outputs. Its bluntness is its limit: the bias applies to a token in every context, and it works on tokens, not words or meanings.

concepts.label: Logit Lens
concepts.def_snippet: An interpretability technique that reads a transformer's intermediate predictions by projecting hidden states from each layer through the model's output head, revealing how its guess about the next token forms and changes across depth. It is near-free, borrowing the model's own vocabulary projection so it speaks in tokens the model knows, and it rests on a basis-consistency assumption the tuned lens exists to repair.

concepts.label: Logits
concepts.def_snippet: The raw, unnormalized scores a model emits for every token at each step, before a softmax converts them into a probability distribution. They are the single surface every decoding and sampling control acts on, temperature divides them, truncation reads the probabilities they produce, a penalty subtracts from them, so understanding sampling means seeing it as a sequence of edits to this one vector before it is normalized.

concepts.label: Long Context
concepts.def_snippet: A model's ability to process very long inputs, from tens of thousands to millions of tokens, enabling whole-document and multi-document reasoning. It is hard for three converging reasons, quadratic attention, a ballooning KV cache, and scarce long-reasoning training data, and the honest catch is that the advertised window often exceeds the effective one, with recall sagging in the middle.

concepts.label: Long Short-Term Memory
concepts.def_snippet: A recurrent network built around a protected cell state that information can ride along almost untouched, with learned input, forget, and output gates deciding what to write, keep, and read. The design exists to give the gradient a route through time that is added to rather than repeatedly multiplied, the multiplication that destroys memory in a plain recurrent network.

concepts.label: long-context reasoning
concepts.def_snippet: Long-context reasoning is the capability of a language model to process and draw inferences across very large inputs, such as long documents or extended conversations, addressing t…

concepts.label: Long-Horizon Reasoning
concepts.def_snippet: Reasoning and acting coherently across many steps toward a goal far from the starting point, where each step depends on earlier ones and an early mistake can doom everything after. It is what separates an agent that finishes a long task from one that drifts, and the place where errors compound most punishingly.

concepts.label: LoRA
concepts.def_snippet: A parameter-efficient method that freezes a pretrained model and injects small, trainable low-rank weight matrices, so a large model is adapted by training a tiny fraction of its parameters. It rests on the observation that the weight change needed to specialize a model has low intrinsic rank, and once trained the low-rank product can be merged back into the frozen weights for zero added inference latency.

concepts.label: Loss Function
concepts.def_snippet: The single number that says how wrong a model is, the quantity training drives down. It is where you specify what the model should do, and its quiet danger is that a model optimizes exactly what you measure: a loss that fails to reward the behavior you actually want produces a model that does the wrong thing perfectly.

concepts.label: Loss Landscape
concepts.def_snippet: The high-dimensional surface you get by plotting a model's loss over every setting of its parameters, the terrain training walks across. Its geometry decides how hard optimization is, and the surprising empirical fact is that the landscapes of huge networks are unusually benign: most minima are about equally good, and the real obstacle is saddle points, not bad valleys.

concepts.label: Loss of Plasticity
concepts.def_snippet: The gradual erosion of a network's ability to learn new things under continued training: a network that learns well at first becomes progressively unable to fit new data, even with its capacity unchanged. It is the flip side of catastrophic forgetting (losing old knowledge versus losing the ability to acquire new), and it surfaces most sharply in reinforcement learning's non-stationary targets.

concepts.label: Lottery Ticket Hypothesis
concepts.def_snippet: The conjecture that a randomly initialized dense network already contains a small subnetwork which, trained alone from its original initialization, matches the full network's accuracy. Its deeper implication is that overparameterization helps mainly by buying more "lottery tickets", a search advantage in finding a trainable solution, not a representational necessity.

concepts.label: Low-Rank Approximation
concepts.def_snippet: Replacing a large matrix with the product of two much smaller ones that captures most of its content, on the bet that the matrix's real information lives in far fewer dimensions than its size suggests. It is the mathematical engine behind PCA, recommender systems, model compression, and the LoRA method for cheap fine-tuning.

concepts.label: Machine Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A branch of artificial intelligence in which a system improves at a task by fitting patterns in data instead of following hand-written rules. It answers a specific obstacle: most of what we know how to do, we cannot say how we do, so we supply examples and let the program recover the rule.

concepts.label: Machine Translation
concepts.def_snippet: The automatic translation of text between natural languages, the task whose pursuit kept forcing the inventions, the encoder-decoder, the attention mechanism, the transformer itself, that then reshaped all of NLP. Its measurable quality (BLEU against human references) made it a demanding testbed that drove rapid progress.

concepts.label: Mamba
concepts.def_snippet: A selective state space model that makes the recurrence input-dependent, so the model decides token by token what to remember and what to ignore, and runs in linear time via a hardware-aware parallel scan instead of quadratic attention. Selectivity is the whole point: it recovers attention's content-based routing that fixed state space models lacked.

concepts.label: Manifold
concepts.def_snippet: A space that looks flat and ordinary up close, even when its global shape is curved or twisted. The idea matters because real high-dimensional data is believed to fill not its whole space but a low-dimensional manifold inside it, so learning a good representation is, in effect, the act of discovering that surface and finding coordinates for it.

concepts.label: Manifold Hypothesis
concepts.def_snippet: The conjecture that real high-dimensional data does not fill its space but concentrates on a low-dimensional manifold inside it, governed by a handful of genuine degrees of freedom. It is the load-bearing assumption under representation learning: if it holds, the right thing to learn is coordinates for that surface, which is exactly what an autoencoder does.

concepts.label: Manifold Optimization
concepts.def_snippet: Optimization where the variables are pinned to a smooth manifold, done by stepping along the manifold's own geometry so every iterate satisfies the constraint exactly, rather than penalizing or projecting after the fact. It is how you optimize the many naturally constrained objects in ML, unit directions, rotations, covariances, low-rank and orthogonal matrices, without fighting the constraint.

concepts.label: Market Dynamics
concepts.def_snippet: The economic forces shaping the AI industry: who funds the enormous training runs, how labs compete and price, where value accrues across the stack, and whether the spending is justified by returns. The discourse theme that tracks AI as a business and a market, not only a technology.

concepts.label: Markov Chain
concepts.def_snippet: A model of a system that hops between states where the next state depends only on the present one, not the whole history. That single memoryless assumption is what makes otherwise intractable sequences analyzable, and its stationary distribution, the long-run fraction of time in each state, turns out to be an eigenvector, the same math behind MCMC sampling and PageRank.

concepts.label: Markov Chain Monte Carlo
concepts.def_snippet: A family of algorithms that sample a hard distribution by simulating a Markov chain engineered so its long-run equilibrium is that very distribution. Its magic is that it never needs the normalizing constant, because the algorithms depend only on density ratios in which the constant cancels, which is what makes intractable Bayesian posteriors computable. It is exact in the limit, the slow-but-faithful opposite of variational inference.

concepts.label: Markov Decision Process
concepts.def_snippet: The mathematical framework that formalizes sequential decision making under uncertainty, defined by states, actions, transition probabilities, and rewards, the standard model on which reinforcement learning is built. Its load-bearing assumption is the Markov property, that the present state is a sufficient summary of the past, which is exactly the condition that lets the Bellman equation decompose a long trajectory into a one-step recursion.

concepts.label: Mask R-CNN
concepts.def_snippet: A landmark computer-vision architecture that detects each object in an image, draws a box around it, and predicts a pixel-level mask of its exact shape, all in one network. It unified object detection and instance segmentation, and its key technical fix, aligning features to exact pixel coordinates, made the masks sharp enough to become a standard tool.

concepts.label: Masked Autoencoder
concepts.def_snippet: A self-supervised method that hides most of an image's patches and trains a model to reconstruct the missing content, learning rich visual features from unlabeled images. Two choices make it work: a very high masking ratio (around 75%, far above language's, because images are so spatially redundant) and an asymmetric design where the heavy encoder sees only the visible quarter, making pretraining several times cheaper.

concepts.label: Masked Language Modeling
