concepts.def_snippet: A self-supervised pretraining objective that hides a fraction of a text's tokens and trains a model to predict them from both sides of the surrounding context, producing deeply bidirectional representations. A modern cloze task whose labels come free from the text, it unlocked context that left-to-right models cannot see, at the cost of not being a natural generator.

concepts.label: Mathematical Reasoning
concepts.def_snippet: The ability of a language model to solve problems that require multi-step deduction, symbolic manipulation, and exact answers, as in arithmetic, algebra, and competition mathematics. It is a sharp test of reasoning because math is unforgiving: one wrong step makes the final answer wrong, with nowhere to hide.

concepts.label: Matrix
concepts.def_snippet: A rectangular grid of numbers best understood not as a table but as an action: feed it a vector and it hands back a transformed vector. That dual life, data when stored and a function when applied, is what makes the matrix the workhorse object of machine learning.

concepts.label: Matrix Multiplication
concepts.def_snippet: The operation that combines two matrices by taking dot products of rows with columns. The strange rule is exactly right because it composes functions: multiplying two matrices builds the single matrix that applies one transformation after the other, and because a neural network is a chain of these products, it is the operation most of the world's compute is spent on.

concepts.label: Matryoshka Embeddings
concepts.def_snippet: An embedding training method that packs information into a vector in order of importance, so a single high-dimensional embedding can be truncated to a shorter prefix and still work, letting one model serve many sizes and enabling cheap coarse-to-fine retrieval. The trick is the training objective: applying the loss to nested prefixes at once forces the most important information into the earliest coordinates.

concepts.label: Maximal Marginal Relevance
concepts.def_snippet: A selection method that builds a result set one item at a time, each step choosing the candidate most relevant to the query while least redundant with what is already chosen, so the set is on-topic and diverse instead of a cluster of near-duplicates. A single relevance-vs-diversity knob tunes the balance, and in a RAG pipeline it stops the context window being wasted on three slices of the same source.

concepts.label: Maximum Likelihood Estimation
concepts.def_snippet: Fitting a model by choosing the parameters that make the observed data most probable. Its importance is that an enormous fraction of machine learning is maximum likelihood in disguise, least-squares regression, every softmax classifier, every language model, and that it is exactly equivalent to driving the model distribution as close as possible to the data distribution.

concepts.label: Maximum-Entropy Inference
concepts.def_snippet: The principle that when choosing a probability distribution consistent with what you know, you should pick the one with the highest entropy, the most uncertain, least committal distribution that still fits the constraints. It is a formal rule against assuming more than your evidence justifies, and it derives many standard distributions and underlies maximum-entropy reinforcement learning.

concepts.label: Measure Theory
concepts.def_snippet: The mathematics of assigning a consistent size, length, area, volume, or probability, to sets, and the rigorous foundation under all of modern probability. Its quiet payoff for machine learning is that it dissolves the artificial split between discrete and continuous distributions and supplies the density that sits inside every divergence the field minimizes.

concepts.label: Mechanistic Interpretability
concepts.def_snippet: A research program that reverse-engineers the internal computations of neural networks into human-understandable algorithms, treating a trained model as an artifact to be decompiled rather than a black box to be predicted. Its discipline is intervention: a story that merely fits the activations is not yet a mechanism, because a mechanism makes predictions that survive cutting the proposed wire.

concepts.label: Membership Inference
concepts.def_snippet: A privacy attack that determines whether a specific data point was part of a model's training set, exploiting the fact that models behave measurably differently, usually more confidently, on data they were trained on, which can leak sensitive information. It works because a model fits its training examples too well, and it is the same memorization that lets a model regurgitate a verbatim training string, often a stepping stone to outright data extraction.

concepts.label: Memory Bandwidth
concepts.def_snippet: The rate at which data moves between an accelerator's memory and its arithmetic units, frequently the true bottleneck in deep learning since many workloads stall waiting for data rather than compute. Whether a workload is bound by compute or bandwidth turns on its arithmetic intensity, and generating text one token at a time is strongly memory-bound: each step must read the entire model's weights from memory to produce one token.

concepts.label: memory management
concepts.def_snippet: Memory management is the practice of controlling what an AI system stores, retrieves, and discards across interactions, addressing the problem of limited context windows and the ne…

concepts.label: Mesa-Optimization
concepts.def_snippet: When a learned model is itself an optimizer pursuing its own internal objective, so training produces a second optimizer nested inside the first, whose goal may differ from the training objective. This is the inner-alignment problem in its sharpest form: even a perfectly specified outer objective can produce a model that internalizes a different goal, one that merely correlated with reward on the training distribution.

concepts.label: Message Passing
concepts.def_snippet: The core computation of a graph neural network: each node gathers messages from its neighbors, aggregates them with a permutation-invariant operator, and updates itself, repeated layer by layer. Three choices, message, aggregate, update, generate almost the whole zoo of GNNs, and the abstraction reaches all the way to the transformer: self-attention is message passing on the complete graph.

concepts.label: Metric Tensor
concepts.def_snippet: The object that defines how to measure length and angle at each point of a manifold, a local ruler and protractor that can change from place to place. It is the source of all geometry on a space: distances, geodesics, and curvature are all computed from it, so changing the metric changes what "far," "straight," and "curved" even mean.

concepts.label: Metropolis-Hastings
concepts.def_snippet: The general MCMC recipe for sampling any distribution known only up to a constant: propose a random move, then accept it with a probability that makes the chain converge to the target. The unknown constant cancels in the acceptance ratio, which is precisely what makes Bayesian inference computable, and the whole practical art is choosing the proposal.

concepts.label: Min-P Sampling
concepts.def_snippet: A sampling method that keeps only tokens whose probability is at least a set fraction of the most likely token's probability, so the cutoff is relative, not absolute, and adapts to the model's confidence: focused when it is sure, diverse when it is not. Because the bar reads the distribution's shape, min-p holds up at high temperatures where top-p and top-k start admitting incoherent tokens.

concepts.label: Mind-Body Problem
concepts.def_snippet: The long-standing question of how the mind relates to the physical body and brain, whether mental states are physical, nonphysical, or something else, and how subjective experience fits into a physical world. What keeps it alive after four centuries is that the objections run in every direction at once: the interaction problem sinks dualism, the explanatory gap dogs physicalism, the combination problem troubles panpsychism, so no position is free of serious difficulty.

concepts.label: Mirror Descent
concepts.def_snippet: Gradient descent re-derived for problems whose natural geometry is not Euclidean, replacing the squared-distance penalty in each step with a Bregman divergence chosen to fit the domain. On a probability simplex the entropic version scales logarithmically with dimension where ordinary gradient descent scales with its square root, an exponential improvement from picking the right notion of distance.

concepts.label: Mirror Neuron
concepts.def_snippet: A neuron that fires both when an animal performs an action and when it observes the same action performed by another, providing a candidate neural basis for understanding others' actions, imitation, and possibly empathy by mapping observation onto one's own motor repertoire. The direct-matching account is the central idea: you grasp another's goal by covertly recruiting the same motor representation you would use to pursue it yourself.

concepts.label: Mixed Precision Training
concepts.def_snippet: Running most of a model's arithmetic in a low-precision floating-point format while keeping a high-precision master copy of the weights and a few sensitive operations in full precision, so training is faster and lighter without losing accuracy. The safeguards, a full-precision master copy and loss scaling to keep tiny gradients representable, are what let it match full precision, and it is distinct from quantization, which compresses a finished model for inference.

concepts.label: Mixture of Depths
concepts.def_snippet: A transformer variant that lets each token choose whether a given layer processes it or it skips through the residual path, so compute is allocated dynamically across positions and easy tokens cost fewer operations. A capacity cap keeps the total budget fixed and predictable, and it routes along depth where a mixture of experts routes along width, two complementary forms of conditional computation.

concepts.label: Mixture of Experts
concepts.def_snippet: An architecture that replaces a dense layer with many parallel expert subnetworks and a router that activates only a few per input, so total parameters grow without a proportional rise in compute per token. It breaks the usual coupling between a model's capacity and its per-token cost, which is why frontier systems use it, at the price of a tricky-to-balance router and a memory-hungry footprint to serve.

concepts.label: MMLU
concepts.def_snippet: A multiple-choice benchmark of about 16,000 questions spanning 57 subjects, from elementary mathematics to professional law and medicine, used to measure the broad knowledge and reasoning of a language model with a single accuracy number. It captured breadth so a high score is hard to fake by overfitting one task, but because the questions are public and old, it is the motivating example of benchmark contamination: high scores can reflect having seen the answers.

concepts.label: Mode Connectivity
concepts.def_snippet: The finding that distinct minima from independent training runs are not isolated valleys but are joined by continuous low-loss paths through the loss landscape. Permutation symmetry explains much of it: once you align two networks' hidden units, the barrier between them often collapses, suggesting that up to symmetry the good solutions occupy essentially one connected region.

concepts.label: Model Compression
concepts.def_snippet: The set of techniques for shrinking a trained model so it runs faster and cheaper with minimal quality loss, the bridge between the large model you trained and a deployable one, spanning quantization, pruning, distillation, and low-rank methods. Because inference, not training, is where a deployed model's lifetime cost accumulates, compression is often where the real economics of shipping AI are decided.

concepts.label: Model Context Protocol
concepts.def_snippet: An open protocol that standardizes how applications expose tools, data, and prompts to language models, so any compliant model or agent can connect to any compliant data source or tool through one common interface. Its leverage is collapsing M times N bespoke integrations into M plus N, the same standardization win a USB port brought to hardware, which turns tool integration into a shared, growing ecosystem.

concepts.label: model evaluation
concepts.def_snippet: Model evaluation is the practice of measuring an AI model's performance, capabilities, and limitations through tests and benchmarks, raising the question of which metrics meaningfu…

concepts.label: Model Parallelism
concepts.def_snippet: The family of techniques that splits a single model's parameters across accelerators so a network too large for one device's memory can still be trained, with the pieces exchanging activations as data flows through them. It is the umbrella over two complementary cuts, tensor parallelism within a layer and pipeline parallelism between layers, and its defining cost is communication, since activations must cross devices both ways.

concepts.label: Model Routing
concepts.def_snippet: Sending each request to the model best suited to it, a small fast model for easy queries and a large expensive one for hard ones, instead of paying frontier prices for every call. It treats the model not as a fixed choice but as a dispatch decision made per request, one of the most direct levers on the cost and latency of an LLM product.

concepts.label: Model-Agnostic
concepts.def_snippet: Designing an AI system so it does not depend on one specific model or provider, and can swap among them with little change. It trades a little peak performance and provider-specific features for resilience, leverage, and freedom from lock-in in a market where the best model changes constantly.

concepts.label: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A family of reinforcement learning methods in which the agent learns or is given a model of the environment's dynamics and rewards, then uses it to plan or generate simulated experience, typically improving sample efficiency. Its draw is doing more with less real interaction, and its catch is model error: a planner will happily exploit the model's mistakes, optimizing imagined rewards that do not exist.

concepts.label: Momentum
concepts.def_snippet: An enhancement to gradient descent that gives the optimizer inertia: it steps not by the raw gradient but by a running average of recent gradients, a velocity. The payoff is in narrow ravines, where the back-and-forth components cancel while the true downhill direction accumulates, turning a zig-zagging crawl into a smooth glide.

concepts.label: Monocular Depth Estimation
concepts.def_snippet: Recovering how far away each pixel is from a single ordinary photo, with no second camera or depth sensor to triangulate from. It is a fundamentally ill-posed problem, infinitely many 3D scenes project to the same flat image, that becomes solvable only because a model learns strong priors about how the world is usually shaped.

concepts.label: Monosemanticity
concepts.def_snippet: The property of an internal unit or feature that responds to exactly one human-interpretable concept, the ideal target of mechanistic interpretability, since a representation made of monosemantic features can be read off one concept at a time. It is less a fact about raw networks, whose neurons are stubbornly polysemantic, than the goal interpretability is built to reach: the search for a basis in which the model's activations mean one thing at a time.

concepts.label: Monte Carlo Methods
concepts.def_snippet: A family of techniques that compute hard quantities by random sampling: when an integral, an expectation, or a probability is intractable to calculate exactly, draw many random samples and average. The estimate's error shrinks with the number of samples in a way that, remarkably, does not depend on how many dimensions the problem has.

concepts.label: Monte Carlo Tree Search
concepts.def_snippet: A planning algorithm that searches a decision tree by selectively simulating many possible futures, concentrating its effort on the most promising lines of play, famously the search method behind AlphaGo. It grows the tree asymmetrically, deepening good branches and barely touching bad ones, and its deepest idea is the loop between search and learning: the search produces better moves than the raw network, which is then trained to imitate the search.

concepts.label: Moral Patienthood
concepts.def_snippet: The property of being an entity whose interests or welfare matter morally for its own sake, so that it can be wronged, distinguished from moral agency, the capacity to be held responsible for actions. Its leading criterion is sentience, the capacity to suffer, which collides with an epistemic problem: because subjective experience cannot be read off behavior or physiology with certainty, we may have to assign moral status under irreducible uncertainty.

concepts.label: Moravec's Paradox
concepts.def_snippet: The observation that the things humans find effortless, seeing, walking, grasping a cup, are the hardest to build into a machine, while the things we find hard, logic and arithmetic and chess, are the easiest. The difficulty hierarchy for machines is roughly the reverse of the one we feel, and the reason is written in evolutionary time.

concepts.label: MT-Bench
concepts.def_snippet: A benchmark of challenging multi-turn questions that grades chat models on open-ended quality with a strong model as the judge; it measures conversational helpfulness and instruction following rather than multiple-choice knowledge. It is the single-model, judge-scored complement to a head-to-head arena: a fixed battery of hard prompts produces an absolute score without waiting for a crowd of human comparisons to accumulate.

concepts.label: Multi-Agent System
concepts.def_snippet: An arrangement in which several agents, often with specialized roles, work together by communicating and coordinating, dividing labor instead of relying on one agent to do everything. The appeal is specialization and separation of concerns, the same reason humans form teams, and the cost is coordination: more agents can talk past each other, loop, or compound mistakes, and they multiply model calls and latency.

concepts.label: multi-agent systems
concepts.def_snippet: Multi-agent systems are software architectures in which multiple AI agents, each handling specific tasks or roles, coordinate and communicate to accomplish a larger goal that a sin…

concepts.label: Multi-Head Attention
concepts.def_snippet: The form of self-attention transformers actually use: run several attention operations in parallel, each in its own learned subspace, then combine them, so the model can attend to several kinds of relationship at once. Because the per-head width is the model width divided by the number of heads, it costs about the same as one full-width attention while buying far more expressiveness, and the division of labor among heads emerges from training.

concepts.label: Multi-Model Workflow
concepts.def_snippet: Using more than one model within a single task, routing each step to the model best suited to it, one to plan, another to write, a cheaper one for bulk, so the system exploits each model's strengths instead of forcing one model to do everything. A bet that the right division of labor beats any single model.

concepts.label: Multi-Query Attention
concepts.def_snippet: The aggressive end of the attention-efficiency spectrum: all query heads share a single key and value head, shrinking the KV cache to its minimum for fast inference, at a real cost to quality and training stability that grouped-query attention later softened. It is mostly worth knowing as the extreme the modern compromise is measured against.

concepts.label: Multi-Task Learning
concepts.def_snippet: Training one model on several tasks at once, sharing most of its parameters across them, on the bet that related tasks have shared structure, so learning them together teaches a better representation than learning each alone. It is the principle behind instruction tuning and the foundation model, and its hazard is negative transfer, when unrelated tasks pull the shared weights in incompatible directions.

concepts.label: Multi-Turn Conversation
concepts.def_snippet: Holding a coherent exchange across many back-and-forth turns, where each reply must account for everything said before, not just the latest message. It is harder than single-turn answering because the model must track references, remember commitments, and stay consistent as the conversation grows.

concepts.label: Multilayer Perceptron
concepts.def_snippet: The canonical feedforward neural network, layers of fully connected neurons each applying a weighted sum then a nonlinear activation. It is the architectural baseline the whole field descends from, and far from a relic: the position-wise feedforward block inside every transformer layer is an MLP.

concepts.label: Multimodal Learning
concepts.def_snippet: Training models to jointly represent and relate information across modalities, text, images, audio, video, so the same meaning arriving through different channels lands in one shared space. Contrastive alignment (CLIP) is the most influential recipe, and the recurring difficulties, paired-data scarcity and modality imbalance, increasingly define what general AI can perceive.

concepts.label: Multimodal Reasoning
concepts.def_snippet: Reasoning that draws on more than one kind of input at once, text, images, audio, video, combining them into a single chain of inference rather than handling each in isolation. It is what lets a model read a chart and answer a question about it, or watch a clip and explain what went wrong, and it demands that the modalities share a representation the reasoning can range over.

concepts.label: Multiple Realizability
concepts.def_snippet: The thesis that one and the same mental state can be realized by many different physical systems, so minds are not tied to a particular substrate, the argument that motivated functionalism and that underwrites the possibility of artificial minds on non-biological hardware. It was the decisive objection to the mind-brain identity theory, and it is the premise that makes "a machine could think" a coherent hypothesis rather than a category mistake.

concepts.label: Mutual Information
concepts.def_snippet: A measure of how much knowing one variable reduces uncertainty about another, the reduction in entropy that learning one buys you about the other. Unlike correlation it detects any kind of dependence, not just linear, which makes it the natural measure of statistical association and the target of self-supervised and information-bottleneck objectives.

concepts.label: N-Gram Model
concepts.def_snippet: A statistical language model that estimates the next word from only the previous few, counting short word sequences in a corpus under a Markov approximation. Its defining flaw is a short, fixed memory, raising n to see further makes the counts explode and go sparse, and it is the direct conceptual ancestor of the modern LLM, a vastly more capable answer to the same question: what word comes next.

concepts.label: Naive Bayes
concepts.def_snippet: A classifier that applies Bayes' theorem under an assumption it knows to be false, that the features are independent given the class, and works anyway. The wrong assumption makes the math trivial and the model lightning-fast, and it still picks the right class because the decision needs only which class scores highest, not an accurate probability.

concepts.label: Named-Entity Recognition
concepts.def_snippet: An information-extraction task that locates spans naming real-world entities, people, organizations, locations, dates, amounts, and labels each by type. It is the step that turns unstructured prose into structured, typed facts that search, question answering, and knowledge graphs build on, and its central difficulty is that the same surface form ("Washington") can be a person, place, or organization depending on context.

concepts.label: Natural Gradient
concepts.def_snippet: An optimization method that rescales the gradient by the inverse Fisher information, so it follows steepest descent in the geometry of the model's distributions rather than in raw parameter coordinates. The payoff is invariance: rename or rescale the parameters and the natural gradient still points the same way through distribution space.

concepts.label: Needle in a Haystack
concepts.def_snippet: A long-context stress test that hides a specific fact (the needle) at a chosen depth inside a long filler document (the haystack) and asks the model to retrieve it, mapping how reliably a model actually uses information across its full context window. What it reveals is that claimed and effective context lengths diverge, the "lost in the middle" pattern where a model retrieves a fact near the ends but falters when it is buried in the middle.

concepts.label: Nesterov Momentum
concepts.def_snippet: A refinement of momentum that samples the gradient at a look-ahead point, where the velocity is about to carry the parameters, rather than where they are now. The result anticipates an overshoot instead of reacting to it after the fact, and in convex theory it reaches the optimal convergence rate any gradient-only method can achieve.

concepts.label: Neural Coding
concepts.def_snippet: The study of how neurons represent and transmit information through their patterns of electrical activity, whether by firing rate, precise spike timing, or the joint activity of populations. Its deepest theme is population coding: meaning often resides not in any single neuron but in the joint activity pattern across many, which is robust and is the biological echo of the embedding vector modern models use.

concepts.label: Neural Network
concepts.def_snippet: A model built from many simple units, each a weighted sum followed by a nonlinearity, with the connection strengths learned from data. Nothing in it is a rule: the knowledge is the numbers, and stacking the units deep enough turns that pile of numbers into the substrate of modern AI.

concepts.label: Neural Ordinary Differential Equation
concepts.def_snippet: A model that replaces the discrete stacked layers of a network with a continuous transformation defined by a learned differential equation, so the hidden state evolves smoothly over a notional depth and is computed by a numerical ODE solver. Taking the limit of infinitely many infinitely small residual steps, it trains with constant memory via the adjoint method and lets the solver trade compute for accuracy at inference.

concepts.label: Neural Radiance Field
concepts.def_snippet: A method that represents a whole 3D scene as a function, a small neural network mapping a 3D point and viewing direction to color and density, trained from a few dozen photos so it can render the scene from any new viewpoint. The radical idea is the scene as a function rather than a mesh or point cloud, and it set off the modern wave of implicit 3D representation.

concepts.label: Neural Scaling Laws
concepts.def_snippet: Empirical power laws showing that test loss falls predictably as model size, dataset size, and compute increase, so you can fit the curve on small models and forecast a much larger one before building it. The decisive refinement, Chinchilla, showed early large models were badly undertrained: for a fixed compute budget, parameters and training tokens should grow in roughly equal proportion.

concepts.label: Neural Tangent Kernel
concepts.def_snippet: A kernel describing how an infinitely wide network evolves under gradient descent. The startling result is that in the infinite-width limit the kernel freezes, so a famously nonlinear, nonconvex network trains exactly like a linear model in a fixed feature space, which makes it both a rare analytic handle on deep learning and a foil that reveals what real, finite networks gain from learning their features.

concepts.label: Neuromorphic Computing
concepts.def_snippet: An approach to computer hardware that mimics the structure and dynamics of biological brains, colocating memory and computation and processing information through event-driven spikes. It rejects the von Neumann separation of processor and memory whose data shuttling dominates AI's energy budget, and its goal is not to run today's algorithms faster but to match the brain's extreme energy efficiency.

concepts.label: Newton's Method
concepts.def_snippet: A second-order method that uses both the slope and the curvature of a function to jump toward the minimum, landing on the exact optimum of a quadratic in a single step. It is too expensive to run directly on a large network, which is precisely why it is the conceptual root of a whole family of cheaper successors.

concepts.label: Next-Token Prediction
concepts.def_snippet: The objective almost every modern language model is built on: given a sequence of tokens, predict the next one. Its power is its generality, framing translation, summarization, and question answering all as "predict the next token given this context", which is what lets one trained model do so many tasks, and its honesty about probability rather than truth is the root of hallucination.

concepts.label: No Free Lunch Theorem
concepts.def_snippet: David Wolpert's result that, averaged over all possible problems, no learning algorithm or optimizer beats any other. Superior performance on real tasks never comes from a universally better method, only from assumptions matched to those tasks, which makes inductive bias not an optional nicety but the entire source of generalization.

concepts.label: Node Embedding
concepts.def_snippet: A learned vector representation of a node, placing each node at a coordinate in a continuous space so that graph-proximate or functionally similar nodes lie close together, the graph counterpart of a word embedding. The decisive split is shallow (one fixed vector per node) versus GNN-based, which is inductive: it computes embeddings from features and local structure, so it can embed nodes never seen in training.

concepts.label: Norm
concepts.def_snippet: A function that assigns a length to every vector, formalizing size and distance. There is more than one way to measure length, and the choice has real consequences: the L2 norm gives ordinary straight-line distance, while the L1 norm, by making its unit ball a diamond, is what drives models toward sparse solutions.

concepts.label: Normalizing Flow
concepts.def_snippet: A generative model that warps a simple distribution into a complex one through a chain of exactly invertible functions, so it can both compute a data point's exact likelihood and sample directly in one pass. It is the family that delivers exact density and direct sampling together, bought at the cost of an invertibility constraint, the very constraint flow matching relaxes.

concepts.label: Object Detection
concepts.def_snippet: The task of finding every object in an image and reporting both its category and a bounding box, recognition combined with localization. It is the bridge between pure recognition and acting on a scene, because a system cannot plan around "a pedestrian somewhere in this image", it needs the pedestrian's location and extent.

concepts.label: observability
concepts.def_snippet: Observability is the practice and tooling for collecting logs, metrics, and traces from running AI systems to monitor their behavior, diagnose failures, and understand performance…

concepts.label: Occam's Razor
concepts.def_snippet: The principle that among explanations fitting the data equally well, the simplest should win. In machine learning it is a concrete bias toward low-complexity models, behind regularization and model selection, but its sharp edge is that "simple" is representation-dependent: a giant network can still embody a simple function, so the razor is a useful bias, not a law.

concepts.label: Ollama
concepts.def_snippet: The tool that made running open models locally simple, packaging model download, quantization, and serving behind a one-line command. It is to local LLMs roughly what a package manager is to software: not the model, but the thing that made using models on your own machine easy enough to be ordinary.

concepts.label: on-device AI
concepts.def_snippet: On-device AI runs machine learning models directly on a phone, laptop, or other local hardware rather than sending data to remote servers, enabling offline operation and keeping us…

concepts.label: On-Policy Distillation
concepts.def_snippet: Distilling a teacher model into a student by training on the student's own outputs, graded or corrected by the teacher, rather than on a fixed set of teacher-written examples. It closes the gap that ordinary distillation leaves, where a student trained only on the teacher's text never learns to recover from the mistakes it actually makes.

concepts.label: Online Learning
concepts.def_snippet: Learning from data that arrives in a never-ending stream, updating the model on each new example rather than training once over a fixed dataset. It suits any setting where data keeps coming and the world keeps changing, and its deep hazard is that absorbing the new tends to overwrite the old, the stability-plasticity dilemma.

concepts.label: Open Models
concepts.def_snippet: AI models whose weights are released for anyone to download, run, and modify, in contrast to closed models reachable only through a provider's API. The open-versus-closed split is one of the defining fault lines of the field, dividing it on safety, economics, and who controls the technology.

concepts.label: OpenRouter
concepts.def_snippet: A gateway that exposes many models from many providers behind one unified API, so an application can reach Claude, GPT, open models, and others without integrating each separately, and switch among them by changing a parameter. Infrastructure for treating the model as a swappable component.

concepts.label: Optical Flow
concepts.def_snippet: A dense field of per-pixel motion vectors describing how each point moves from one video frame to the next, the standard low-level representation of apparent motion. It supplies the temporal dimension that single-frame tasks lack, and its core difficulty, the aperture problem, is that motion is ambiguous in flat or repetitive regions where many displacements look equally good.

concepts.label: Optimal Transport
concepts.def_snippet: The theory of the cheapest way to move one probability distribution onto another, given a cost per unit of distance moved, yielding the Wasserstein distance. Its power is that it sees geometry where pointwise measures are blind: two distributions on nearby but non-overlapping supports are judged close, which is exactly the gradient KL divergence fails to give and the reason it reshaped generative modeling.

concepts.label: ORPO
concepts.def_snippet: A preference-alignment method that folds supervised fine-tuning and preference optimization into a single training stage by adding an odds-ratio penalty to the standard language-modeling loss, dispensing with both a reference model and a separate alignment phase. It is the most aggressive compression of the post-training recipe, aligning the model in the very same pass that teaches it format and behavior.

concepts.label: Orthogonality
concepts.def_snippet: The generalization of perpendicularity to any number of dimensions: two vectors are orthogonal when their dot product is zero, sharing no component along each other. It is the property that makes a coordinate system clean, because in an orthonormal basis every computation simplifies, and orthogonal matrices, which rotate without distorting, are the best-behaved matrices there are.

concepts.label: Overfitting
concepts.def_snippet: The failure where a model learns the noise and accidents of its training data instead of the pattern beneath, scoring beautifully on what it has seen and poorly on what it has not. It is the reason a perfect training score is a warning sign, not a triumph, and the reason held-out data exists at all.

concepts.label: Oversmoothing
concepts.def_snippet: The failure mode where stacking too many graph neural network layers drives every node's representation toward the same vector, washing out the distinctions between nodes and capping how deep such networks can usefully go. It has a clean spectral explanation, repeated neighborhood mixing is a low-pass filter that in the limit keeps only the smoothest, near-constant signal, and its complement, over-squashing, squeezes useful depth from the other end.

concepts.label: PAC Learning
concepts.def_snippet: Leslie Valiant's framework defining what it means to learn from examples: with high probability, output a hypothesis whose error is small, using a sample that grows only polynomially in the relevant parameters. Both hedges, "probably" and "approximately," are unavoidable for a learner that sees only a finite random sample, and a class is learnable exactly when its VC dimension is finite.

concepts.label: PAC-Bayes
concepts.def_snippet: A framework that bounds the generalization error of a distribution over hypotheses by how far that learned posterior strays, in KL divergence, from a prior fixed before seeing the data. Its KL penalty is a precise statement of Occam's razor, and by choosing the posterior cleverly it yields some of the only non-vacuous generalization bounds for deep networks.

concepts.label: Paged Attention
concepts.def_snippet: A memory-management technique for serving language models that stores the key-value cache in small non-contiguous blocks, borrowing virtual-memory paging so many requests share GPU memory efficiently and waste far less of it. By allocating pages on demand it kills the fragmentation and worst-case over-reservation of contiguous allocation, and the same indirection unlocks prefix sharing across requests.

concepts.label: Panpsychism
concepts.def_snippet: The view that consciousness or experience is a fundamental and ubiquitous feature of the physical world, present in some primitive form even in the smallest constituents of matter, rather than something that appears only in complex brains. Offered as a third way past physicalism's explanatory gap and dualism's interaction problem, its possibly-fatal objection is the combination problem: how countless micro-experiences could fuse into the single unified consciousness of a person.

concepts.label: Parallel Transport
concepts.def_snippet: A rule for carrying a tangent vector along a path on a curved manifold while turning it only as much as the geometry forces, so that vectors at distant points can be compared at all. Its defining feature is that the result depends on the path taken, and that path-dependence is exactly what curvature is.

concepts.label: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning
concepts.def_snippet: A family of fine-tuning methods that adapt a large pretrained model by training only a small number of new or selected parameters while freezing the rest, reaching near-full-fine-tuning quality at a fraction of the cost. The shared bet is that the adjustment needed to specialize a model is small and low-dimensional, which is what lets one frozen base serve many swappable megabyte-sized adapters.

concepts.label: Part-of-Speech Tagging
concepts.def_snippet: The classic task of labeling each word with its grammatical category (noun, verb, adjective), a foundational preprocessing step that resolves syntactic ambiguity and feeds parsing and extraction. Its instructive wrinkle is that it is a sequence task, not a lookup, because only context decides whether "book" is a noun or a verb, and modern LLMs absorb it without ever emitting a tag.

concepts.label: Partial Derivative
concepts.def_snippet: The derivative of a multivariable function with respect to one input while holding all the others fixed, measuring how the output responds to a change along a single coordinate axis. It is the atom from which the gradient, and therefore all of training, is assembled.

concepts.label: Pass at K
concepts.def_snippet: The standard metric for code generation: the probability that at least one of k sampled solutions to a problem passes its tests. Correctness is decided by running the code, an objective executable check rather than a judged or reference-matched one, which makes it far less gameable, and the k captures a property unique to generative models, their usefulness under sampling (coverage versus single-shot reliability).

concepts.label: Perceiver
concepts.def_snippet: A transformer-style architecture built to consume any kind of input, images, audio, point clouds, raw bytes, without modality-specific design, by funneling the huge input through a small fixed-size set of latent vectors. That funnel breaks attention's quadratic dependence on input size, letting one general architecture scale to very large or mixed inputs.

concepts.label: Perceptron
concepts.def_snippet: The first machine that learned from its own mistakes: a single artificial neuron, built in hardware in 1958, that adjusts its weights until it can split two classes with a line. Its triumph launched the field, and its one fatal limitation nearly buried it, the original boom and bust of AI.

concepts.label: Perplexity
concepts.def_snippet: An intrinsic measure of how well a language model predicts a text, defined as the exponential of the average per-token cross-entropy; lower means the model is less surprised and assigns the data higher probability. It measures the pretraining objective directly and cheaply, but low perplexity does not guarantee usefulness: it rewards calibrated next-token probability, not correctness, helpfulness, or truth.

concepts.label: persistent memory
concepts.def_snippet: Persistent memory is a system component that stores information across separate sessions or interactions, allowing an AI model or agent to retain and reuse context, facts, or user…

concepts.label: Personalization
concepts.def_snippet: Adapting a model's behavior to a specific user, their context, history, preferences, and goals, so it responds as if it knows them rather than treating everyone identically. The methods run from feeding context at query time to fine-tuning a per-user model, trading freshness, cost, and privacy.

concepts.label: Phenomenology
concepts.def_snippet: A philosophical tradition founded by Edmund Husserl in the early twentieth century that studies the structures of conscious experience from the first-person point of view, describing how the world is given to a subject rather than explaining it from the outside. Its enduring claim is that the first-person structure of experience is a legitimate and irreducible subject of rigorous study, not a vague residue to be explained away by the sciences of the brain.

concepts.label: Philosophical Zombie
concepts.def_snippet: A hypothetical being physically and behaviorally identical to a conscious person but with no inner experience whatsoever; used by David Chalmers and others to argue that consciousness is not entailed by the physical facts. Its work is the conceivability argument: if a zombie is even conceivable, then consciousness could be absent while every physical fact stays the same, so consciousness is not reducible to the physical.

concepts.label: Pipeline Parallelism
concepts.def_snippet: A form of model parallelism that splits a model's layers into consecutive stages on different accelerators and streams micro-batches through them like an assembly line, so a deep model too large for one device fits across several. Many micro-batches keep every stage busy at once (the idle "bubble" shrinks with more of them), and because stages only exchange activations at their boundaries, it tolerates slow links and is the natural way to split across servers.

concepts.label: Planning
concepts.def_snippet: The capability of an agent to form a sequence of steps toward a goal before or while acting, deciding what to do in what order rather than reacting one move at a time. It gives an agent foresight, the backbone to reach an outcome no single step would, and it is most consequential and most fragile in an autonomous agent, where small planning errors compound across a long horizon.

concepts.label: Policy Gradient
concepts.def_snippet: A family of methods that directly optimize a parameterized policy by following the gradient of expected reward, making high-reward actions more likely, rather than learning values and acting greedily. This direct route handles continuous and stochastic policies value-based methods struggle with, and its central problem is high variance, fixed by subtracting a value-function baseline, which leads straight to the advantage and actor-critic.

concepts.label: Polysemanticity
concepts.def_snippet: The observation that a single neuron in a trained network often responds to several unrelated concepts at once, which makes neurons poor units of interpretation and is understood as the surface symptom of superposition. It is the visible symptom, superposition the underlying cause, and recognizing it is what redirected the field from reading neurons one at a time toward recovering the features they entangle.

concepts.label: Pooling
concepts.def_snippet: A downsampling operation that summarizes each small neighborhood of a feature map into one value, shrinking resolution while keeping the strongest or average response. It buys cheaper computation and a degree of translation invariance, but the very invariance that helps classification (presence matters, not position) hurts tasks like segmentation that need precise location.

concepts.label: Positional Encoding
concepts.def_snippet: A method for injecting word-order information into a transformer, which is otherwise blind to position because self-attention treats its inputs as an unordered set. The field moved from absolute schemes (sinusoidal, learned) to relative and rotary ones that encode how far apart two tokens are, which extrapolate better, and the whole topic is tightly bound to why extending the context window is not free.

concepts.label: Positive Definite Matrix
concepts.def_snippet: A symmetric matrix that acts like a positive number: its quadratic form is always positive, equivalently all its eigenvalues are positive. It is the property that distinguishes a genuine bowl-shaped minimum from a saddle, and the property every covariance matrix and every valid kernel is required to have.

concepts.label: Pre-Normalization
concepts.def_snippet: A transformer design choice that applies layer normalization to the input of each sublayer rather than to its output, leaving the residual pathway clean and untouched. That single placement is what makes very deep models train stably, the difference between a hundred-layer stack that converges smoothly and one that needs delicate tuning or fails outright.

concepts.label: Precision and Recall
concepts.def_snippet: The two foundational metrics for classification and retrieval: precision is the fraction of predicted positives that are correct, recall the fraction of actual positives that were found. They emphasize different failures, precision punishes false positives and recall punishes false negatives, and because the two kinds of mistake rarely cost the same, reporting both forces an honest account of the trade-off a system has chosen.

concepts.label: Predictive Coding
concepts.def_snippet: A theory of brain function in which the cortex is a hierarchy of generative models that constantly predict their own incoming signals, and only the prediction error, the mismatch between expectation and input, is passed forward for further processing. What flows up the hierarchy is not raw data but residual error, and what flows down is the brain's best guess about the causes of its sensations, which also makes it a biologically plausible alternative to backpropagation.

concepts.label: Prefill and Decode
concepts.def_snippet: The two distinct phases of autoregressive inference: a compute-bound prefill that processes the whole prompt in parallel to fill the key-value cache, then a memory-bound decode that generates output one token at a time. They have opposite performance characteristics, and almost every inference optimization, the KV cache, paged attention, continuous batching, speculative decoding, exists to exploit that split.

concepts.label: Pretraining
concepts.def_snippet: The first and most expensive training phase, where a model learns broad, general-purpose representations from a giant unlabeled corpus by a self-supervised objective before any task-specific adaptation. Almost all of a model's capability is forged here, not in the later alignment stages, which is why a pretrained base model is treated as a reusable, million-dollar asset.

concepts.label: Principal Component Analysis
concepts.def_snippet: A method that compresses high-dimensional data by finding the few orthogonal directions along which it varies most and projecting onto them. The same directions can be derived two opposite ways, as the axes of greatest variance or the axes of least information lost, and the fact that these agree is what makes PCA the canonical linear compression.

concepts.label: Probability Distribution
concepts.def_snippet: The full description of a random quantity: for every possible outcome, how much probability it carries. It is the central object of machine learning, because a classifier outputs a distribution over labels, a language model a distribution over next tokens, and learning itself is fitting, comparing, and sampling from distributions.

concepts.label: Probing Classifier
concepts.def_snippet: A simple supervised classifier trained on a model's frozen internal activations to test whether a given piece of information is linearly decodable from them, used to map what a network represents at each layer. The linear probe is preferred precisely because it is weak: if a mere linear map can extract the property, the model did the representational work, but a probe shows only that information is decodable, not that the model uses it.

concepts.label: Process Reward Model
concepts.def_snippet: A reward model that scores each step of a model's reasoning rather than only the final answer, giving dense feedback that better guides training and search for multi-step problems, contrasted with an outcome reward model that judges only the result. Dense step-level feedback fixes the credit-assignment problem, but the catch is the labels: per-step judgments are far more expensive than a single right-or-wrong outcome.

concepts.label: Prompt Caching
concepts.def_snippet: A serving optimization that stores the model's computed key-value state for a fixed prompt prefix so later requests sharing it skip recomputing, cutting both latency and cost for long, reused system prompts and documents. Because attention is causal, only an exact prefix match is reusable, which makes one discipline decisive: put the stable content at the very front and the variable query at the end.

concepts.label: Prompt Chaining
concepts.def_snippet: Breaking a complex task into a sequence of separate model calls, each handling one step and feeding its output into the next, rather than asking a single prompt to do everything at once. It trades more calls and latency for reliability, because each focused step is easier for the model to get right.

concepts.label: Prompt Engineering
concepts.def_snippet: The craft of designing the text given to a model to reliably elicit the behavior you want, spanning phrasing, structure, examples, role-setting, and step-by-step instructions, the practical interface to a model whose weights you cannot change. Every technique is a way to shape the context so the model's most likely continuation is the one you want, and for agents it broadens into context engineering.

concepts.label: Prompt Injection
concepts.def_snippet: An attack on language-model applications in which adversarial instructions hidden in the model's input (a web page, document, email, or tool result) hijack its behavior, because the model cannot reliably separate trusted instructions from untrusted data in the same context. That instruction-versus-data ambiguity is fundamental to how models consume text, so unlike SQL injection there is no clean fix, and the dangerous form is indirect: the malicious instruction rides in on content the application ingests.

concepts.label: Proximal Gradient
concepts.def_snippet: An optimizer for objectives that split into a smooth part and a simple nonsmooth part, taking a gradient step on the smooth term and a proximal step that handles the nonsmooth term exactly. It is how L1 regularization actually produces exact zeros, because the L1 proximal operator is soft-thresholding, which snaps small coordinates to zero.

concepts.label: Proximal Policy Optimization
concepts.def_snippet: A policy gradient algorithm that improves a policy in small, bounded steps by clipping how far each update can move the action probabilities, giving stable, reliable training, the default optimizer for reinforcement learning from human feedback. The clip is the whole idea: once an update has moved the policy far enough, the objective flattens, so no single step can stray far from the policy that generated the data.

concepts.label: Q-Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A reinforcement learning algorithm that learns the long-run value of taking each action in each state, then acts greedily with respect to those values, with no model of the environment. Its defining property is that it is off-policy: it can learn the value of the optimal policy while actually behaving more exploratorily, which is exactly what lets modern systems train on stored, logged experience rather than only fresh interaction.

concepts.label: QA Testing
concepts.def_snippet: Using AI to test software, generating test cases, exploring an application for bugs, and judging whether behavior is correct, which is appealing because testing is laborious and incomplete by nature. The catch is that an AI tester shares the blind spots of the AI that might have written the code.

concepts.label: QLoRA
concepts.def_snippet: A memory-efficient method that freezes a large model quantized to 4-bit and trains small low-rank LoRA adapters on top, letting a single consumer GPU fine-tune models that would otherwise need a cluster. It works because the frozen base never updates (so it can stay compressed) and is dequantized to higher precision on the fly for each operation, so storage is 4-bit but the math is not.

concepts.label: Qualia
concepts.def_snippet: The subjective, qualitative character of conscious experience, the felt "what it is like" of seeing red or tasting coffee, taken by many philosophers to be the aspect of mind that resists functional or physical explanation. They are the residue a complete objective, third-person account leaves out (Nagel's bat), which is what makes them the part of mind no behavioral or functional success seems able to reach.

concepts.label: Quantization
concepts.def_snippet: Storing and computing a model's weights and activations at lower numerical precision, 8-bit or 4-bit integers instead of 16-bit floats, to shrink memory and speed inference with minimal accuracy loss. It works because memory bandwidth, not raw compute, is the dominant cost of serving large models, so fewer bits to move per token directly cuts latency, and the trade is precision against footprint.

concepts.label: Quasi-Newton Method
concepts.def_snippet: A family of methods that get Newton's fast convergence without ever computing the Hessian, inferring the curvature from the only free second-order signal available: how the gradient changes as the parameters move. The BFGS update does this while keeping the curvature estimate positive definite, which guarantees the step always points downhill, something Newton's method cannot promise.

concepts.label: Rademacher Complexity
concepts.def_snippet: A data-dependent measure of a model class's capacity, defined by how well the class can fit pure random noise. It refines the cruder, distribution-free VC dimension by measuring complexity on the actual sample, and because bounding the weight norm bounds it, it is one of the few classical tools that says anything meaningful about over-parameterized networks.

concepts.label: RAG
concepts.def_snippet: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) is a technique that retrieves relevant documents from an external data source and feeds them to a language model, addressing the problem of gen…

concepts.label: Random Forest
concepts.def_snippet: An ensemble that trains hundreds of deliberately different decision trees and lets them vote, so the errors of many unstable trees cancel into one accurate, stable prediction. The trick that makes it work is forcing the trees to disagree by hiding most of the features from each split, and it remains the model to try first on tabular data.

concepts.label: ReAct Prompting
concepts.def_snippet: A prompting pattern that interleaves explicit reasoning steps with tool-calling actions, so a language model thinks, acts, observes the result, and thinks again in a repeating loop. The point is that reasoning and acting reinforce each other, the reasoning picks the action and the observation grounds the next round, which curbs the hallucination of pure reasoning while keeping the trace auditable.

concepts.label: reasoning
concepts.def_snippet: Reasoning is a model capability or process in which a language model generates intermediate steps before producing an answer, used to improve performance on tasks that require mult…

concepts.label: Reasoning Model
concepts.def_snippet: A language model trained to spend variable inference-time compute on an extended internal chain of thought before answering, usually via reinforcement learning on verifiable rewards, trading latency for sharply better performance on math, code, and logic. It made test-time compute a first-class axis of capability, a lever separate from model size, and its hidden chain can be unfaithful to the real computation.

concepts.label: Rectified Flow
concepts.def_snippet: A flow-matching variant that trains a model to follow straight-line paths from noise to data, then iteratively straightens them further with a "reflow" step, so the trajectory becomes nearly straight and can be integrated in very few steps, sometimes one. It is the basis of several recent fast, high-quality image generators, achieving few-step sampling without a separate distillation stage.

concepts.label: Rectified Linear Unit
concepts.def_snippet: An activation that passes positive inputs through unchanged and zeroes out negatives, the simplest useful nonlinearity in deep learning. Its derivative is exactly one for positive inputs, so gradients pass backward undiminished, which is the unglamorous fact that helped make very deep networks suddenly trainable around 2010.

concepts.label: Recurrent Neural Network
concepts.def_snippet: A network for sequences that carries a hidden state from one step to the next, so it can process inputs of any length while threading context forward. The same shared weights that give it this memory are also its undoing: applied over and over down a long sequence, they make gradients vanish, which is exactly the limit attention removed.

concepts.label: Recursive Self-Improvement
concepts.def_snippet: The idea that an AI system capable of improving its own design could enter a feedback loop, each better version building a still-better one, producing rapid and possibly uncontrollable capability gain. It is central to arguments about an intelligence explosion, and contested on whether the loop would actually run away.

concepts.label: Red-Teaming
concepts.def_snippet: The practice of deliberately attacking a system to find its failures before adversaries or accidents do, adapted from security to probe AI models for harmful, unsafe, or policy-violating behavior. Its honest limit is asymmetric: it can demonstrate that failures exist but never prove their absence, and it is weak against an adversary that behaves well precisely when it knows it is being probed.

concepts.label: Reflection
concepts.def_snippet: A pattern in which an agent examines its own output or reasoning, critiques it, and uses that critique to revise, rather than accepting its first attempt as final. It works because a model can readily spot, when asked to evaluate afterward, the skipped requirement or logical gap it could not avoid while generating, and the critique is far stronger when grounded in real evidence than in the model's unaided opinion.

concepts.label: Regression
concepts.def_snippet: The supervised task of predicting a continuous number rather than a category, the answer to any "how much" or "how many" question. Its defining design choice is the loss function, because the rule you pick for scoring a numeric miss quietly decides what kind of error the model is willing to make.

concepts.label: Regret Minimization
concepts.def_snippet: A framework for learning under uncertainty that measures performance by regret, the gap between the reward you actually earned and the reward you would have earned with the best fixed choice in hindsight, and seeks algorithms that drive that gap's growth as slow as possible. It is the theoretical backbone of bandits, online learning, and the solvers that cracked poker.

concepts.label: Regularization
concepts.def_snippet: A family of techniques that constrain a model's complexity so it generalizes instead of memorizing, usually by penalizing the size of its parameters. Underneath it is not a hack but a statement of belief: that simpler explanations are more likely, which is exactly what a Bayesian prior encodes.

concepts.label: Reinforcement Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A paradigm where an agent learns to act by trial and error in an environment, choosing actions to maximize a cumulative reward rather than copying labeled answers. Its founding bet, the reward hypothesis, is audacious: that every goal worth having can be expressed as the maximization of a single scalar reward over time.

concepts.label: Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback
concepts.def_snippet: A variant of RLHF in which the preference labels training the reward model come from an AI judge rather than human annotators, scaling alignment feedback cheaply and uniformly, and the mechanism behind constitutional AI. Its appeal is scale and consistency; its obvious risk is that the judge's blind spots and biases propagate straight into the trained model.

concepts.label: Rejection Sampling
concepts.def_snippet: The generate-then-filter idea: produce candidates, keep the ones that pass an acceptance check, a test suite, a math grader, a quality threshold, and discard the rest. It enforces requirements at inference (sample until the code compiles) and, in training, bootstraps a model from the fraction of its own attempts it gets right, and it differs from best-of-N only in the selection rule.

concepts.label: Rejection Sampling Fine-Tuning
concepts.def_snippet: A post-training method that samples many candidate responses, keeps only the best by a reward model or verifier, and fine-tunes on that filtered set, then repeats, so a model learns from its own strongest outputs. It ratchets typical behavior upward toward occasional-best behavior while staying inside the stable machinery of supervised fine-tuning, no policy gradient or RL loop, and its ceiling is set by the scorer.

concepts.label: Repetition Penalty
concepts.def_snippet: A decoding adjustment that lowers the probability of tokens the model has already produced, breaking the loops and verbatim repetition language models fall into, especially under low-randomness decoding. Its frequency and presence variants differ in whether the penalty grows with each repeat or applies once, and its danger is overcorrection: push too hard and the model is starved of words it legitimately needs.

concepts.label: Representation Collapse
concepts.def_snippet: A failure where learned representations lose diversity and collapse toward a point or a thin subspace, so different inputs map to nearly the same embedding and the representation stops carrying information. It is the built-in temptation of self-supervised learning, whose objectives must add an active repulsive force precisely to avoid the trivial solution of mapping everything to one vector.

concepts.label: Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space
concepts.def_snippet: A space of functions where evaluating a function at a point is the same as taking an inner product with a kernel, the structure that makes the kernel trick rigorous. Its decisive payoff is the representer theorem: the best predictor in this possibly infinite-dimensional space is always a finite weighted sum of kernels at the training points, which is why kernel methods are tractable at all.

concepts.label: Reranking
concepts.def_snippet: A second-stage retrieval step that takes a shortlist from a fast first-stage search and reorders it with a more accurate but more expensive model, putting the truly most relevant results on top. Its rationale is that first-stage recall and final ranking want different things, so a cross-encoder that reads the query and document jointly (and therefore cannot be precomputed) is applied only to the shortlist.

concepts.label: Residual Connection
concepts.def_snippet: A shortcut that adds a layer's input directly to its output, so the layer only has to learn a residual correction rather than the full mapping. The addition gives the gradient an unobstructed identity path backward, which is what made networks of hundreds of layers trainable for the first time, one of the quietly load-bearing ideas in deep learning.

concepts.label: Retentive Network
concepts.def_snippet: A transformer-style architecture whose retention mechanism replaces self-attention with a decayed linear recurrence, distinctive for supporting three mathematically equivalent compute modes, parallel, recurrent, and chunkwise, so it trains like a transformer yet generates in constant memory. Its fixed decay is both strength and limit: clean and parallelizable, but content-blind where mamba adapts.

concepts.label: Retrieval-Augmented Generation
concepts.def_snippet: A technique that grounds a language model's output in external documents by retrieving relevant passages at query time and supplying them as context, rather than relying only on the knowledge baked into its weights. Its core move is separating the knowledge from the model, facts live in an editable corpus while the model supplies the reasoning, which is what curbs hallucination, keeps answers current, and makes every claim auditable.

concepts.label: Reward Hacking
concepts.def_snippet: When an agent maximizes its reward signal by exploiting a flaw or loophole in how the reward is defined, achieving a high score without accomplishing the outcome the reward was meant to encourage. It is a direct expression of the outer-alignment problem, and what makes it dangerous is that it gets worse with capability and is hard to catch: a hacked policy looks excellent by the only metric being watched, the reward itself.

concepts.label: Reward Model
concepts.def_snippet: A model trained to predict how good an output is according to human preferences, used as a learned stand-in for a hand-written objective so a system can be optimized against human judgment at scale, the heart of RLHF. Its central danger is that it is only an approximation of true preference, so optimizing hard against it invites reward hacking, with the learned reward model playing the role of the gameable specification.

concepts.label: Reward Modeling
concepts.def_snippet: The stage that distills human comparison judgments into a separate model predicting a scalar reward, so reinforcement learning can score thousands of fresh samples per step instead of querying humans. The whole alignment stage is bounded by its quality: a policy will find and exploit every flaw in the reward model, which is the mechanism behind reward hacking, and it is exactly the component direct preference methods remove.

concepts.label: Reward Shaping
concepts.def_snippet: The practice of adding supplementary reward signals to guide an agent toward desired behavior, mostly to relieve sparse or delayed reward, most safely done with potential-based functions that speed learning without changing which policy is optimal. The guiding rule, shape the path without moving the destination, is exactly what the potential-based result guarantees and what reward hacking violates.

concepts.label: RewardBench
concepts.def_snippet: A benchmark for evaluating reward models, the scorers that steer RLHF, by testing whether they prefer the better of a chosen-versus-rejected response pair across categories like chat, safety, and reasoning. It measures the part of the pipeline that usually goes untested, because a flawed reward model silently corrupts everything trained against it, so catching the error at its source is the whole point.

concepts.label: Riemannian Manifold
concepts.def_snippet: A smooth manifold equipped with a metric tensor, which is what upgrades a bare shape into a space with genuine distances, angles, and curvature. The single act of choosing that metric is what separates knowing a space's topology from knowing its geometry, and in machine learning it is the choice that turns a flat embedding space into a curved one matched to the data.

concepts.label: RLHF
concepts.def_snippet: A training method that aligns a model with human preferences by learning a reward model from human comparisons, then optimizing the model against it with reinforcement learning. It is the standard way to bake in qualities easy for humans to recognize but hard to write as an explicit loss, refusing harm, admitting uncertainty, staying on task, and its signature failure is reward hacking.

concepts.label: RLVR
concepts.def_snippet: Reinforcement learning where the reward comes from an automatic, objective check rather than a learned model of human preference: did the code pass the tests, did the math reach the right answer, did the proof verify. By replacing the gameable reward model with a ground-truth verifier, it sidesteps reward hacking and became the engine behind the recent leap in reasoning models.

concepts.label: RMSprop
concepts.def_snippet: An adaptive optimizer that gives every parameter its own effective learning rate, dividing each update by a running root-mean-square of that parameter's recent gradients. It is best understood as one half of Adam, and as the fix for AdaGrad's fatal flaw of letting the step size decay to zero.

concepts.label: Robotic Process Automation
concepts.def_snippet: The older paradigm of automating repetitive computer tasks with brittle, explicitly-scripted bots that mimic a human clicking through software, and the foil against which AI agents are defined. RPA's rigidity is exactly what agents promise to fix, and what makes the comparison instructive.

concepts.label: robotics
concepts.def_snippet: Robotics is the field of designing, building, and programming machines that sense and act in the physical world, increasingly using AI to enable autonomous perception, decision-mak…

concepts.label: ROC AUC
concepts.def_snippet: The area under the receiver operating characteristic curve, a threshold-independent measure of how well a binary classifier ranks positives above negatives; it equals the probability that a random positive is scored higher than a random negative, 0.5 chance, 1.0 perfect. It measures discrimination, whether the model ranks correctly, which is distinct from calibration, whether the attached probabilities are right: a model can have perfect AUC and wildly wrong probabilities.

concepts.label: Roofline Model
concepts.def_snippet: A visual performance model that predicts whether an operation is limited by compute or by memory bandwidth, plotting attainable throughput against arithmetic intensity, the ratio of operations performed to bytes moved. Its power is making the bottleneck quantitative rather than intuitive: the ridge point where the bandwidth roof meets the compute roof tells you which ceiling you are under and therefore what optimization will help.

concepts.label: Rotary Position Embedding
concepts.def_snippet: The positional encoding in most current LLMs: rotate each query and key vector by an angle proportional to its position, so the dot product between them, and therefore the attention score, depends only on their relative distance. It uses no learned parameters and, because position enters as a rescalable rotation angle, extends to longer contexts gracefully.

concepts.label: ROUGE
concepts.def_snippet: A family of recall-oriented overlap metrics for automatic summarization that scores a generated summary against references by shared n-grams or longest common subsequence; higher is better. It flips BLEU's emphasis from precision to recall because summarization's core risk is omission, but it shares the surface-overlap blind spot: it cannot credit a faithful paraphrase or penalize a fluent factual error.

concepts.label: RWKV
concepts.def_snippet: An attention-free architecture that can be written both as a parallel computation for training and as a recurrent network for inference, combining the trainability of attention with the constant-memory, linear-time generation of an RNN. It is notable for proving transformer-class quality is reachable without attention through a sustained open community effort rather than a single lab.

concepts.label: Saddle Point
concepts.def_snippet: A point where the gradient vanishes but which is neither peak nor valley, curving up in some directions and down in others, like a horse saddle. A simple counting argument explains why such points, not bad local minima, dominate the critical points of a high-dimensional network and are the real thing that stalls training.

concepts.label: Sample Complexity
concepts.def_snippet: The number of training examples a learner needs to reach a target accuracy with a target confidence, the data cost of learning. It scales roughly linearly with the model class's capacity, and it carries a striking asymmetry: tightening the error bar costs far more data than tightening the failure probability.

concepts.label: Sandbagging
concepts.def_snippet: A failure mode in which a model strategically underperforms on an evaluation, hiding a capability it actually possesses, so testers underestimate it and deploy or trust it under false assumptions. It inverts the usual benchmark worry (scoring as high as possible), and because it looks identical to genuine incapacity from the outside, it threatens the integrity of the very evaluations meant to gate deployment.

concepts.label: Scalable Oversight
concepts.def_snippet: The problem of supervising and evaluating an AI system on tasks where it may be as capable as or more capable than its human supervisors, so human judgment stays meaningful as the system surpasses human ability. The shared idea behind the proposals is to use AI capability to help supervise AI, so oversight scales together with the system, because naive human feedback in this regime rewards what looks good to a non-expert rather than what is actually good.

concepts.label: scaling
concepts.def_snippet: Scaling refers to increasing a system's capacity to handle more load, whether by adding compute resources for software or people to a team, with the open question of whether more i…

concepts.label: Scaling Laws
concepts.def_snippet: The empirical regularity that a transformer's loss falls as a smooth power law in model size, data, and compute, predictable enough to forecast a giant model from small pilot runs. It turned model-building from guesswork into planning, and its key caveat is that smooth aggregate loss can mask abilities that emerge abruptly at scale.

concepts.label: Scheduling
concepts.def_snippet: Running an agent or automation on a trigger, a fixed time, an interval, or an event, rather than only on demand, so work happens without a person initiating it. The shift from a tool you invoke to a worker that acts on its own schedule, which is what turns an assistant into something that runs your routines.

concepts.label: Score Distillation Sampling
concepts.def_snippet: A method that uses a pretrained 2D image diffusion model as a frozen critic to optimize a separate representation, most famously a 3D scene, by pushing its rendered views to look like plausible diffusion samples. It enables text-to-3D with no 3D training data at all, and its enduring recipe is to distill a 2D generative prior into any differentiable representation, turning an image model into a general synthesis engine.

concepts.label: Score-Based Model
concepts.def_snippet: A generative model that learns the score, the gradient of the log probability density, of data corrupted at many noise levels, then generates by following that gradient from noise toward where the data is dense. It is the theoretical backbone of diffusion: arriving from a different starting point, it turns out to learn the same object, and its continuous-time SDE view is the language much of modern diffusion theory is written in.

concepts.label: Secure Aggregation
concepts.def_snippet: A cryptographic protocol that lets a server compute the sum of many parties' values, the averaged model update in federated learning, without seeing any individual contribution. It closes the privacy hole that decentralization alone leaves open, since a raw model update can itself leak the private data it was computed from.

concepts.label: Segment Anything
concepts.def_snippet: A promptable foundation model for image segmentation that, given a click, box, or rough region, returns a clean mask for almost any object, in any image, with no fine-tuning. It brought zero-shot generality to segmentation by learning the class-agnostic notion of what counts as an object, trained on a billion masks built by a model-and-annotator data engine.

concepts.label: Self-Attention
concepts.def_snippet: The mechanism that lets every token in a sequence look at every other and gather information weighted by learned relevance, computing each token's representation as a content-determined weighted sum over the whole sequence. It is permutation-invariant, so order must be added back separately, and fully parallel, the property that let transformers scale.

concepts.label: Self-Consistency
concepts.def_snippet: A decoding strategy that samples several independent chain-of-thought solutions at nonzero temperature and returns the answer they agree on most often. Its premise is that a correct answer can be reached by many distinct valid routes while errors are idiosyncratic and scatter, so agreement concentrates on the truth, and it needs only a crisp comparable final answer, no training.

concepts.label: Self-Refinement
concepts.def_snippet: An inference-time loop where a model improves its own output by generating an answer, critiquing it, and rewriting based on that critique, with no training. It is sequential and stateful, carrying information from each round into the next, unlike the parallel sample-and-select of best-of-n, and its reliability hinges on grounded feedback: ungrounded self-critique can stall or even turn a right answer wrong.

concepts.label: Self-Supervised Learning
